<!-- ===== source: Facilitator Resources/Learning Self-Assessment.md ===== -->

---

# Learning Self-Assessment

| | |
|---|---|
| **For** | The learner (filled by you, for you), held lightly by the CM in anonymized aggregate |
| **When** | Part 1 at enrollment and again after Day 10 · Part 2 before each High module (Days 5, 6, 7, 9) |
| **What it is** | A self-verification instrument. Not a quiz. Not scored. No badge. No pass, no fail. |
| **Reads alongside** | `01 - Course Architecture and Pedagogy.md` (the learning outcomes this restates) · `04 - Container and Gatekeeping Protocol.md` §L (how the aggregate feeds the post-cohort review) |

PM is verified in lived experience, not multiple choice (Doc 02 §J). This instrument matches that. Every statement here is something you check against **your own direct experience** — the course's core instruction is to verify each distinction in your own body, not to take anyone's word for it (Doc 01, pedagogical premise §5). So this is not a test you pass. It is a mirror you look into, twice, and notice what moved.

**There is no score.** You will not add anything up. The *delta*, what shifted between the start and the end, is the only signal, and it is a signal for you first. A distinction that is "not yet" at Day 10 is not a failure; it is information about where your direct experience hasn't landed it yet, which is exactly the kind of honesty the work runs on.

**The rating scale (three words, used everywhere in this file):**

- **not yet:** I can't locate this in my direct experience. Maybe I understand the idea, but I don't *have* it in my body.
- **starting:** I catch glimpses. Sometimes, in some moments, I can find it. It's not reliable yet.
- **landed in my body:** I can find this in my direct experience, more or less on purpose. It's mine, not borrowed.

A note on "landed in my body": this is not "I understand it intellectually." The whole course is built against the trap of swallowing PM into "interesting ideas I now know about" (Doc 01, five-body method). If you can explain a distinction cleanly but can't *feel* it or *use* it in a live moment, that's **starting**, not landed. Be honest in the direction of "not yet." The honesty is the practice.

---

## Part 1 — Enrollment baseline and Day-10 re-rate

Rate each statement twice: once now, at the start, and once after Day 10. Don't look at your start answers when you re-rate — answer fresh, then compare. These eleven statements restate the course's per-day learning outcomes (Doc 01, the learning arc) in the first person.

Mark one box per column per row.

| # | Statement (rate against your direct experience) | Start: not yet / starting / landed | Day 10: not yet / starting / landed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **(Day 1: context, radical responsibility)** I can catch the moment I'm about to react from my old default, and choose a different *context* to act from instead, not just a different behavior. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 2 | **(Day 2: Box, thoughtware)** I can notice my Box make a move in real time — a survival reaction running, and see it as the Box, not as *me*. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 3 | **(Day 3: liquid state, center/ground/bubble, five bodies)** I can center, ground, and set a bubble on purpose, and feel the shift when my Box loosens into liquid state. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 4 | **(Day 4: feedback, experiments)** I can take a piece of feedback without defending, and turn it into a small specific experiment I actually run in my life. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 5 | **(Day 5: feelings vs emotions, Numbness Bar)** I can tell a clean *feeling* (present, in my body, no story) from an *emotion* (about something, with a story attached), and I can locate a small amount of a feeling (5%) without becoming it. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 6 | **(Day 6: mixed emotions, emotional healing process)** I can notice when more than one feeling is present at once, and let a feeling move through rather than managing it away. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 7 | **(Day 7: Low Drama, gremlin food)** I can catch myself stepping into a Victim, Persecutor, or Rescuer move, and notice the gremlin food I get from it. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 8 | **(Day 8: listening, speaking, completion loops)** I can listen to someone without fixing, advising, or planning my reply, and I can close a conversation cleanly instead of leaving it open and leaking. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 9 | **(Day 9: ego states, problem ownership)** I can tell which part of me is speaking (Parent, Child, Adult, Gremlin) and choose to come back to Adult instead of collapsing into one of the others. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 10 | **(Day 10: bright principles, three powers)** I can declare something and stand on it (rather than wish or intend it), and I can tell when I'm sourcing from a bright principle versus from fear. | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |
| 11 | **(Whole-course stance)** When discomfort comes up, I can tell the difference between discomfort that is moving (keep going gently) and discomfort that is stuck or too much (ground, pause, reach for support). | ☐ ☐ ☐ | ☐ ☐ ☐ |

### Reading your own delta (for you, no one grades this)

After Day 10, lay the two columns side by side. You are looking for *movement*, not for a row of "landed."

- **Rows that moved one or two steps:** that's the thoughtware installing. That's the course working as designed.
- **Rows still at "not yet":** not a failure. Either that distinction needs more lived reps after the cohort (the work continues in your own life, per Doc 01), or it's a distinction your direct experience genuinely hasn't met yet. Both are honest. Name which it is.
- **A row that went *backward*** (landed → starting): this happens and it's often a *good* sign: you knew less than you thought at the start, and now you can feel the edge of what you actually have. PM calls this kind of honesty an upgrade, not a regression.
- **The biggest mover:** notice it. That's where this cohort did its clearest work in you.

Nothing here goes to anyone with your name on it. If you choose to share your deltas with the cohort, that's the Day 10 closure work (Playbook §8), not this form.

---

## Part 2 — Per-HIGH-module readiness self-check

Before each High module (Days 5, 6, 7, 9), answer a short stem honestly, *to yourself*, before you start. This is not the safety unlock checklist (that's Doc 04 §D: partner reachable, 90 minutes, not in crisis; your CM gates that separately). This is a *learning*-readiness check: does the equipment the next module depends on actually exist in your direct experience yet?

The questions cascade on purpose. Each High module assumes the prior distinctions are at least partly installed (Doc 01, "how distinctions cascade"). If you answer "not yet" to a foundation question, that's not a stop sign. It is a signal to revisit the earlier module's practice, slow your cadence, or take it to your partner before you go in. The honest "not yet" here is what catches a gap *before* the module instead of during it.

Rate each line **not yet / starting / landed in my body**, then read the note under each block.

### Before Day 5 — Feelings vs Emotions

- Can I find my center and ground on purpose? (Day 3 equipment)
- Can I tell a *feeling* (present, in my body) from an *emotion* (about something, story attached)?
- Can I locate a small amount (5%) of a feeling in my body without becoming it?
- Can I notice when I've hit my Numbness Bar (the point where I go flat instead of feeling)?

> If "can I ground on purpose" is **not yet**, go back to the Day 3 practice before Day 5: the emotional-body work stands on that equipment (Doc 01, "why embodiment before feelings"). If telling feeling from emotion is **not yet**, that's fine. Day 5 is where you build it; just go in knowing that's the distinction you're there to meet, not one you should already have.

### Before Day 6 — Mixed Emotions and Emotional Healing Process

- Can I name and locate at least one clean feeling in my body? (Day 5)
- Can I let a feeling move through me for a few breaths without managing it away or telling its story?
- Can I notice when more than one feeling is present at the same time?
- Do I know how to ground and pause if a feeling becomes more than I can hold right now? (Doc 03 §D, §E)

> Day 6 is the most demanding module in the course. If locating a single feeling is still **not yet**, you are not ready for *mixed* feelings yet. Revisit Day 5, and tell your partner and CM you're stretching the cadence here. There is no prize for rushing this one. The grounding-and-pause question is the one to be most honest about: if the answer isn't a solid "landed," re-read Doc 03 §D before you start.

### Before Day 7 — Low Drama and Gremlin Food

- Can I feel my own fear, anger, sadness, or joy as energies, rather than only thinking about them? (Days 5–6)
- Can I catch myself *avoiding* a clean feeling by getting busy, blaming, rescuing, or telling a story?
- Can I name what "gremlin food" is for me: the payoff I get from drama, righteousness, or suffering?

> Low Drama is, mechanically, a way of avoiding your own feelings by routing them through someone else (Doc 01, "why emotional before relational"). If feeling your own feelings as energies is **not yet**, you'll likely *see* the Drama Triangle as a concept but not *catch* it in yourself, which is okay for a first pass. Going in, the move to watch for is the avoidance, not the role-name.

### Before Day 9 — Ego States, Problem Ownership

- Can I hold the distinction that my Box is not me, and my Gremlin is not me, that I *have* both and am *neither*? (Day 2, Day 7)
- Can I tell, in a live moment, roughly which part of me is running things?
- Can I come back to Adult (centered, present, choosing) when I notice I've dropped into reaction?

> Ego states (Parent, Child, Adult, Gremlin, Demon) only work if you can already hold Box-vs-Being-vs-Gremlin (Doc 01, distinction 7). If "my Box is not me" is **not yet**, revisit Day 2 and Day 7 before Day 9; otherwise the ego-state map becomes five new labels instead of a live tool. This is also the module where archetypal material runs strongest; the readiness here is as much about being grounded as about being clever.

### How to use a "not yet" on these

A "not yet" on a foundation question is the instrument doing its job: it caught a gap before the module instead of during it. Your move, in order of preference:

1. **Revisit the prior module's embodied practice** (the Solo Centering and Grounding Scripts and the relevant Day file). Often one more rep is all it takes.
2. **Take it to your partner** as your next exchange — speak the edge you're on out loud and be witnessed (this is what the pairing is for).
3. **Stretch your cadence** for the upcoming module (Doc 02 §I). There is no schedule the work has to keep.
4. **Flag it to your CM** if the gap feels bigger than a slow-down can close. Not because you're in trouble, but because the CM can point you to the right resource (and because a repeated readiness gap across the cohort tells the CM something about the module, see below).

None of these is failing. All of them are the course working the way it's meant to: you, verifying in your own direct experience, choosing your own pace.

---

## Part 3 — The anonymized collection flow (operator + CM steps)

Parts 1 and 2 are the learner's mirror. This part is the plumbing that turns the cohort's mirrors into a no-name aggregate without ever attaching a rating to a person. The mechanism matters: a "we promise we won't look" collection is not anonymization. The flow below makes the promise structural.

### The submission mechanism (no-name by construction)

The learner transcribes three things from their own filled copy (never the copy itself, which stays with them):

1. The two columns of Part 1 (start rating and Day-10 rating, eleven rows).
2. Their Part 2 "not yet" answers, by module (which foundation questions were "not yet" before which High module).
3. Optionally: which row was their biggest mover, and one line of comment.

Two collection channels. The operator offers at least one; offering both raises the return rate:

- **Anonymous form.** A web form with **no login, no name field, no email field**, reachable by a single link shared with the whole cohort (so the link itself identifies no one). The form tool must meet the platform privacy minimums (Doc 04 §H) and must be verified, before the window opens, not to log IP addresses or require an account. If the operator's stack cannot meet that bar, use the paper channel instead.
- **Printed drop.** The learner prints or hand-copies the transcription page (the three items above on one sheet, no name line anywhere on the sheet) and mails or hands it in. For fully remote cohorts, the photographed unsigned sheet goes through the anonymous form as an upload rather than through any channel that shows a sender.

### Operator steps (in order)

1. **Announce at Module 11.** One feed post: what is collected (the transcription, not the form itself), why (course revision, never learner evaluation), the channel(s), and the window. State plainly: *submission is optional and anonymous by construction; skipping it has no consequence of any kind.*
2. **Open the window for 7 days** from cohort close. The [Cohort Calendar Template](Cohort%20Calendar%20Template.md) carries the date.
3. **One reminder, mid-window, to the whole cohort.** Never to individuals. The operator must not track who has or has not submitted, and with this mechanism, cannot.
4. **Close the window and compile** the aggregate (format below) before the post-cohort review.
5. **Small-cohort guard.** If fewer than five submissions arrive, report ranges and patterns in prose rather than exact counts, so a rating cannot be triangulated back to a person. If only one or two arrive, fold them into the next cohort's aggregate rather than reporting them alone.

### The aggregate format (what the operator compiles)

One page, three blocks:

**Block 1: Part 1 deltas.** One row per statement, counts only (example numbers shown):

| Part 1 row | Start: not yet / starting / landed | Day 10: not yet / starting / landed | Moved ≥1 step |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (context, responsibility) | 4 / 5 / 1 | 0 / 4 / 6 | 8 of 10 |
| 2 (Box, thoughtware) | … | … | … |

**Block 2: Part 2 readiness gaps.** One row per High module: how many submissions reported a "not yet" on a foundation question before that module, and which question recurred. Example reading: *"6 of 9 arrived at Module 5 with 'ground on purpose' still not yet: that points back at Module 3, not at the learners."*

**Block 3: one paragraph of pattern.** The smallest-delta row, the largest-delta row, the most repeated readiness gap, and any comment-line theme. No interpretation beyond that; the review does the interpreting.

### The feed-forward (where the aggregate goes)

The aggregate, together with the **completion-by-module pull** (how many learners unlocked each module: operator-facing counts from the platform, never published to learners, never turned into streaks, badges, or leaderboards), goes into the post-cohort course-revision review (Doc 04 §L). The two read together on purpose: the completion pull says *where* learners stopped; the aggregate says *what wasn't landing* when they stopped. The review's required output is one concrete improvement for the next cohort, and these two instruments are the cleanest place to find it.

After the review, the operator archives the aggregate (it is already anonymous) and destroys any paper submissions.

---

## How the aggregate feeds the post-cohort review (operator + CM)

This instrument is the **learning axis** of the post-cohort review (Doc 04 §L). The review already reads the action log for safety signal (green/yellow/red flags by module); this adds a signal for whether the *teaching* is landing.

The CM collects only the **anonymized aggregate**, never an individual learner's ratings tied to their name. What the aggregate surfaces:

- **Baseline → Day-10 deltas across the cohort.** If a given outcome (a row in Part 1) barely moved for most learners, that's a content signal: the distinction isn't landing from the module as written. (Per Doc 03 §L step 5: a pattern across multiple learners on one module means the module gets revised before the next cohort. A learning-delta pattern is the gentler, earlier version of that same signal.)
- **Repeated readiness gaps (Part 2).** If many learners hit "not yet" on the same foundation question before the same High module (say, most of the cohort arrives at Day 5 unable to ground on purpose), that points back at Day 3, not at the learners. The gap-catch the review identifies becomes a concrete "strengthen the Day 3 grounding practice" improvement.
- **One concrete improvement.** Doc 04 §L asks for exactly one improvement per cohort. The learning-axis aggregate is one of the cleanest places to find it: the module with the smallest deltas, or the foundation distinction the most learners arrived without.

What the aggregate is **not**: a ranking of learners, a completion metric, or a gate. No learner passes or fails on the basis of these ratings, and the course adds no badge or certificate (Doc 02 §J). The instrument respects the no-quiz/no-score stance completely: its only use, individually, is the learner's own mirror, and its only use, in aggregate, is to tell the operator and CM where the course itself can land better next time.

---

A closing word, learner to self: be honest in the direction of "not yet." The course has no interest in you performing competence, and the gremlin's appetite for looking-good is exactly the thing PM is built to catch (Doc 02 §J). The distinctions you mark "not yet" at Day 10 are not the course's failures or yours. They are the live edges you get to keep walking, in your own life, after the cohort closes. The work was never the badge. It was always the noticing.

---

<!-- ===== source: Facilitator Resources/Partner Emergency Card.md ===== -->

---

# Partner Emergency Card

*One printable page for the partner/witness. Print it when you pair; keep it with your course materials. If you are reading this because your partner is in trouble right now, start at §1.*

---

## 1. If your partner may be in danger: the stop script

Ask one question, plainly:

> **"Are you in immediate danger right now?"**

- **If yes:** contact local emergency services or your country's crisis line AND the CM **immediately**. Do not wait for course protocol. Do not wait for the CM's 24-hour SLA. Stay with your partner in the message thread until they have connected with crisis services.
- **If no:** voice-message the CM the same day. The CM will reach out to your partner directly.

This is the immediate-danger protocol in the partner agreement you signed. Crisis-line numbers are on the **first page of your referral packet**; the international catch-all is **findahelpline.com**.

---

## 2. If your partner names more than the exchange can hold

> If your partner names material that exceeds the module's scope (trauma surfacing, ideation, intent to act on the original event in their adult life) — gently say so. *"What you are describing is bigger than this exchange can hold. I am glad you told me. I am also asking you to message the CM today, and if there is a clinician you work with, them too. I am still with you. And I am being honest about the limits of what I can do here."* Then continue witnessing. The honesty is part of the witnessing.

Say it close to word for word. The script does three things at once: it keeps you present, it routes the material to the right container, and it stays honest about your limits. All three are the witnessing.

---

## 3. The 60-second grounding: read it aloud

If your partner is flooded, dissociating, or spiraling on a live call, read this to them slowly, one line at a time, and wait for them to do each step. (On voice message: record it the same way, with pauses.)

> **Grounding (60 seconds).**
>
> 1. Stop whatever you were doing in the module. Close the video or the document.
> 2. Stand up. Both feet on the floor. Knees soft.
> 3. Breathe out longer than you breathe in, three times. Audible exhale is fine.
> 4. Name three things you can see in the room.
> 5. Name one thing you can hear.
> 6. Place one hand on your sternum, the other on your belly. Notice that your body is here.
> 7. When you have come back, decide: continue, pause for 10 minutes, or stop the module for today.

Then ask them to say out loud which they chose: continue, pause, or stop. Their answer, in their voice, is your signal that they are back enough to choose.

---

## 4. When to hand off

You are a witness, not a clinician. Hand off (and keep witnessing while you do) when any of these is true:

| What you hear or see | Where it goes |
|---|---|
| Immediate danger to themselves or someone else | §1: emergency services / crisis line + CM, now |
| Suicidal ideation without immediate danger | CM the same day; the CM takes it from there |
| Trauma material (flashback, body memory, sensory replay) | Their clinician today + CM today (the §2 script) |
| Still escalating after the grounding script | CM today; do not run the practice again |
| Anything that leaves you over your head | CM. That is the rule: the partner contacts the CM, not the learner's professional supports |

Handing off is part of witnessing. It is not abandoning your partner; it is refusing to improvise care they need from someone qualified.

---

## 5. The three things you never do

1. **Analyze.** No interpreting, no "this sounds like your father," no therapy vocabulary. Naming their experience for them takes the pen out of their hand.
2. **Fix.** No advice, no suggestions, no "have you tried." The urge to fix is the Rescuer wanting its own discomfort to stop.
3. **Minimize.** No "it's probably fine," no "everyone feels that way," no changing the subject. A minimized disclosure does not shrink; it goes underground.

What you do instead: stay. Hold your own center, ground, bubble; your state is the container. Say less than you think you need to. The witness's energy of being present is the medicine.

---

---

# Possibility Team Conversion Guide

| | |
|---|---|
| **For** | The operator and CM, at cohort close |
| **What it is** | The operator-facing companion to Module 11's learner-facing continuation guide: how to help a closing cohort convert into a standing Possibility Team, when to advise against it, and how to frame it in public |
| **Reads alongside** | Module 11 (the learner-facing guide) · [Cohort Calendar Template](Cohort%20Calendar%20Template.md) (the 30/60/90 touchpoints where conversion status is checked) · [Referral List](Referral%20List.md) |

A cohort that ends well often wants to keep going. The Possibility Team is the right vessel for that wanting: a small ongoing group, peer-held, doing PM practice in working contexts. This guide gives the operator the conversion mechanics. The learners get their own version inside Module 11; this file is what the operator holds while they use it.

---

## 1. Canon correction first: Possibility Teams are OPEN

v1's Referral List described Possibility Teams as "by invitation or after Trainer Path entry." **That was wrong, and the wording is corrected in v2.** A Possibility Team is open: anyone can start one, anyone can ask to join one, and no PM credential, trainer status, or invitation is required. Openness is not a detail of the format; it is the format. A team that presents itself as invitation-only has imported a scarcity move into a structure built to be commonly available.

Operator implications:

- Never tell a closing cohort that their team needs permission, certification, or anyone's blessing to exist.
- The team's own public framing must say it is open (section 6).
- A team may still be *small* and may still say "we are full right now, here is when we next open." Capacity is honest; gatekeeping-by-status is not.

## 2. What changes at conversion (say this plainly to the cohort)

The conversion is a real boundary, not a renaming. On one side, the course; on the other, the team.

| | The course (held container) | The Possibility Team (peer-held) |
|---|---|---|
| Holder | A qualified CM with screening, triage SLAs, a backup, and a clinical consult line | The members, jointly. Nobody is on duty. |
| Entry | Five-screen intake, partner agreement, enrollment terms | The team's own declared agreements |
| Safety | Doc 04's full machinery | The members' own equipment, the grounding scripts, the referral list, and the honesty to route out what the team cannot hold |
| Scope | Modules, gated High-intensity work, EHP inside the unlock structure | Practice in working contexts: experiments, witnessing, teach-backs, completion loops. **Not** an EHP venue by default. A team that wants witnessed EHP work names a specific, willing, practiced witness per session, and the trauma carve-out applies exactly as in Module 6. |
| The CM | Present | **Gone.** The CM's absence is the defining structural fact of the team. |

Every member should hear the last row stated without softening. A team that quietly believes the operator is still somehow holding them has not converted; it has just stopped paying.

What carries over: the witness discipline, the three-point reply shape, confidentiality, the four feelings vocabulary, the experiment format, and the relationships themselves. The thoughtware is theirs now. That is what the course was for.

## 3. When NOT to convert (the operator's veto-shaped advice)

The operator cannot forbid graduates from meeting; the content is free and so are they. But the operator's advice carries weight at close, and there are conditions under which the right advice is "not yet" or "not this group":

1. **Unresolved safety routings.** Any member currently paused under the crisis escalation flow, exited for safety, or awaiting a clinician's green light does not join a peer-held team as their next container. Routing them into an unheld group undoes the routing. The team can form without them and openly welcome them later, when the routing resolves. This is the one condition the operator states as a hard line, to the individual privately, never to the group with names attached.
2. **The team is forming to keep receiving care.** If the energy of the conversion conversation is "we don't want to lose being held," the group is asking for a container, not offering each other a team. Point them at the alumni touchpoints, the witness bench (graduates can serve on it), and a future cohort, and let the team idea wait until it is an offer rather than a request.
3. **Fewer than three committed members.** Two people who want to keep exchanging are a continued partnership; bless it as that. A team needs at least three so witnessing can rotate and no pair calcifies into therapist-and-client.
4. **One member is recruiting the others into something else.** A team formed as a funnel for someone's coaching practice, business, or community is a different gameworld wearing the team's name. Name it plainly and decline to assist the framing.

"Not yet" is a complete answer. The 60-day alumni touchpoint exists partly to revisit it.

## 4. The conversion moment (operator steps)

1. **Module 11 raises it; the operator does not pitch it.** The learner guide presents the Possibility Team as one of the named next containers. The operator answers questions and provides this guide's content; the impulse must come from the members.
2. **Ask for a founding declaration, not a sign-up.** Whoever wants the team to exist declares it (to the cohort feed before it closes, or at the end-of-course call): what the team is for, the meeting rhythm, and that it is open. No declaration, no team: a team that nobody will found out loud is condition 2 above.
3. **Hand over cleanly.** The operator gives the founders: this guide's meeting scaffold (section 5), the public-framing language (section 6), the referral list (the team keeps using it), and the course materials links. Then the operator steps back. No standing seat at team meetings, no feed access, no reports.
4. **Check at 30/60/90.** The alumni touchpoints ask, lightly, whether the team is meeting and whether it is still open. That is the operator's entire ongoing role.

## 5. The first three meetings (the scaffold the operator hands over)

Give this to the founders verbatim or adapted. Sixty to ninety minutes per meeting, voice or in person, three to eight people.

**Meeting 1 — Founding.** The team becomes a gameworld on purpose: context, purpose, rules of engagement, entered by choice.
- Check-in round: one feeling, located, with intensity (2 min each).
- The founding declaration, spoken by the founder, then each member declares their own *why I am in*, one sentence, sized to their actual life (3 min each).
- Agree the working agreements out loud. Start from the course's partner agreement, adapted: witness rather than advise · confidence · the immediate-danger protocol stays word for word · tell the team before going silent. Write them down. They are the team's, not the course's.
- Decide rhythm (fortnightly is the durable default), platform, and who convenes the next two meetings (rotate from day one).
- Completion loop to close: *"Are we complete?"* until it is true.

**Meeting 2 — The practice rhythm.** The shape most teams keep for years:
- Check-in round (as above).
- One teach-back: a member teaches one distinction in their own words, five minutes, and is witnessed, not corrected (10 min).
- Experiment reports: each member reports one experiment from their life in the capture format: what I did · what happened · what the next experiment is (3–4 min each).
- One member takes a 10-minute witnessed speaking slot (the Module 8 exchange shape, three-point reply). Rotate the slot each meeting.
- Completion loop to close.

**Meeting 3 — First review.** Same opening and rhythm, shortened, plus:
- A drift check, asked as questions: has advice crept in? Has one person become the helper? Is anyone using the team as their only support? (The last one routes to the referral list, kindly.)
- Recommit or close: each member says plainly whether they are in for the next three months. A team that closes cleanly here, with a completion loop, is a success, not a failure. A gameworld is entered by choice and closed consciously.
- If recommitted: name the next open-door moment: when and how new members can join (section 6).

## 6. Public framing (the words that keep it open)

Whatever the team posts publicly (a line in a PM community space, a note to friends, a message the operator forwards to the next cohort) must carry the openness. A working template:

> We are a Possibility Team: [number] people practicing Possibility Management distinctions in our actual lives: experiments, witnessing, teach-backs. We meet [rhythm, platform/place]. **Possibility Teams are open: no invitation, credential, or prior training is required to start or join one.** We take new members [when/how]; if we are at capacity we will say so and tell you when we next open. To start your own team instead, the course materials are free: [course link].

Two rules inside it: the openness sentence stays, and capacity language ("we are full until March") is the only permitted limit. The operator should decline to circulate any team announcement framed as exclusive, invitation-only, or status-gated, and may quote section 1 of this file as the reason.

# Referral List — Template

> **What this is.** A template the Community Manager (CM) uses to generate the per-learner referral packet at enrollment. It is not a populated list. It supplies the structure, the canonical PM-aware referral pointers, and the verbatim language that must appear in every packet.
>
> **Who maintains it.** The CM. Updated quarterly (see Section 9) and at the start of every cohort.
>
> **When it is used.** At enrollment, after the learner completes intake (Doc 04 Section A). The CM fills in region-appropriate sections from intake data, removes sections that don't apply (e.g., Section 6 only appears if Screen 4 returned yes), and delivers the result as the per-learner packet (Doc 04 Section E) — as a PDF and on the platform — before content access opens.

---

## 0. Screen → section map (which intake screen each section serves)

Doc 04 Section A.2 has five intake screens. Each one, when it returns a flag, requires specific resources in the learner's packet. This table maps every screen to the section(s) here that answer it, so the CM can see coverage at a glance and not ship a packet that is missing the resource a screen demands.

| Intake screen (Doc 04 A.2) | What it flags | Section(s) in this list | Always or conditional |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Screen 1 — Suicidality** | Suicidal ideation, plan, intent | **§2 Crisis lines** (first in packet) + **§4 Licensed clinicians** | §2 always; §4 always |
| **Screen 2 — Trauma & dissociation** | Flashbacks, lost time, dissociation | **§5 Trauma-qualified clinicians** (+ §4) | §5 for any Screen-2 learner; many CMs keep it for all |
| **Screen 3 — Active addiction / compulsion** | Alcohol, drugs, self-harm, food restricting/bingeing, gambling, sex, screens out of control | **§5A Addiction & Recovery resources** (+ §4 for dual diagnosis) | §5A whenever Screen 3 flags; safe to keep for all |
| **Screen 4 — Coercive / unsafe environment** | Safety, housing, children, income at risk if the learner changes behavior | **§6 Domestic violence & legal resources** | §6 only if Screen 4 returned yes |
| **Screen 5 — Mania / psychosis / spiritual emergency** | Mania, psychosis, hallucinations, psychiatric hospitalization | **§4 Licensed clinicians** + **§2 Crisis lines** | both always; clinician confirmation gated per Doc 04 D |

**Honest gaps to note.**
- **Screen 5 has no dedicated "psychiatric / prescriber" subsection.** It is served by the general licensed-clinician directories in §4 plus the crisis lines in §2. If a learner flags Screen 5, the CM should add the learner's own prescriber as the primary contact (per Doc 04 A.2 Screen 5, access is deferred until the prescribing clinician confirms in writing) and use §4 only as backup. A future revision may add a §4 sub-list of psychiatry/med-management directories.
- **§3 (PM-aware coaches) maps to no screen.** It is opportunity-side support, not a gate response. It is never a substitute for the clinician sections a screen requires.
- **§5A (new) is the home for Screen 3.** Before this revision the list had no addiction resource and Screen 3 had nowhere to point. That gap is now closed.

---

## 1. How to populate this template (CM-side notes)

This is the source; the per-learner packet is the output. To generate a packet:

1. **Read the learner's intake responses (Doc 04 Section A.1).** You need country, state/province, primary language, and answers to all five screens (1 suicidality, 2 trauma, 3 addiction/compulsion, 4 coercive environment, 5 mania/psychosis). See the Section 0 map for which section each screen drives.
2. **Save a copy** as `Referral Packet — [Learner Name] — [Cohort ID].md`. Do not edit the source.
3. **Fill in regional sections.** Keep the learner's country first in Section 2.
4. **Remove sections that do not apply.** Screen 4 no → remove Section 6. Screen 2 no → Section 5 is optional (many CMs leave it in). Screen 3 no → Section 5A is optional (the national addiction/recovery pointers are safe to keep for all learners; remove only if you are trimming the packet). Screen 5 yes → add the learner's own prescriber as the primary clinician contact in Section 4 (Doc 04 A.2 requires written prescriber confirmation before access).
5. **Confirm every named coach or clinician is currently practicing and accepting clients** before the packet ships. Stale referrals are worse than no referrals.
6. **Generate the PDF** and store on the platform. Packet must exist before content access opens (Doc 04 Section K).

The verbatim language in Sections 2 and 8 is canonical. Do not paraphrase.

---

## 2. CRISIS LINES (top of every learner's packet)

> This section is **always first** in every per-learner packet. Not buried, not on a back page. First. The learner's own country goes at the top of the list.

### Always-included list

- **United States.** 988 — Suicide and Crisis Lifeline (call or text). Crisis Text Line — text **HOME** to **741741**.
- **United Kingdom.** Samaritans — **116 123** (free, 24/7). SHOUT — text **85258**.
- **Canada.** **9-8-8** — Suicide Crisis Helpline (call or text, 24/7).
- **Australia.** Lifeline — **13 11 14** (24/7). Beyond Blue — **1300 22 4636**.
- **EU general.** **116 123** — Emotional Support Helpline (available in many EU countries; check local availability).
- **Germany.** Telefonseelsorge — **0800 111 0 111** or **0800 111 0 222** (free, 24/7).
- **France.** **3114** — numéro national de prévention du suicide (free, 24/7).
- **International directory.** **findahelpline.com** — point of last resort if the learner's country is not listed.

### Note to the learner (include verbatim in the packet)

> If your country is not listed above, see **findahelpline.com** or search **"[your country] suicide crisis line"** immediately. Do not delay because the list above doesn't have your number — the directories exist precisely for this.

### Verbatim safety instruction (include in every packet, immediately after the crisis-line list)

> **If you are in immediate physical danger to yourself or another person right now, contact the relevant number above OR your country's general emergency line (e.g., 911 in the US, 999 in the UK, 112 in the EU, 000 in Australia). The course is paused while you are safe.**

### CM note (do not include in the learner-facing packet)

Crisis line numbers change. Verify each at every quarterly update (Section 9). The numbers above were canonical at last review; assume nothing.

## 3. PM-AWARE COACHES (template)

> Coaches trained in Possibility Management who can hold 1:1 work for a learner wanting PM-aware support beyond the partner pairing. Not a substitute for licensed therapy (see Section 4).

### Field template

- **Name.**
- **Country / region.**
- **Languages.**
- **Specialty.** (couples, parents, business owners, grief, men's work, women's work, leadership, etc.)
- **Rate.** (per session; sliding scale if available)
- **Contact.** (email, website, or both)
- **PM training level.** (Possibilitator, Trainer, Possibility Lab graduate, Spark Host, etc.)

### Placeholder entries (CM replaces with real coaches)

1. **[Coach Name].** [Country/region] · [Languages] · [Specialty] · [Rate] · [Contact] · [PM training level]
2. **[Coach Name].** [Country/region] · [Languages] · [Specialty] · [Rate] · [Contact] · [PM training level]
3. **[Coach Name].** [Country/region] · [Languages] · [Specialty] · [Rate] · [Contact] · [PM training level]
4. **[Coach Name].** [Country/region] · [Languages] · [Specialty] · [Rate] · [Contact] · [PM training level]
5. **[Coach Name].** [Country/region] · [Languages] · [Specialty] · [Rate] · [Contact] · [PM training level]

### CM note

Coaches are sourced from the PM Trainer Path graduate directory at **possibilitymanagement.org**. Before adding any coach to a packet, confirm:

- Currently practicing (active website, recent activity, responsive to outreach).
- Currently accepting new clients.
- Language matches the learner's primary language.
- Rate range is realistic for the learner's region.

A stale referral — a coach who closed their practice last year, or is at capacity for six months — is worse than no referral. The learner who needed support and got a dead lead can stop reaching.

## 4. LICENSED MENTAL HEALTH PROFESSIONALS (regional, template)

> Default is the appropriate regional directory. Add verified PM-friendly clinicians on top where the CM has them.

### Default — regional directory

The CM enters the directory most appropriate for the learner's region. Common examples:

- **United States / Canada.** Psychology Today (psychologytoday.com).
- **United States.** Therapy Den (therapyden.com) — values-aligned filtering.
- **United Kingdom.** BACP register (bacp.co.uk); UKCP register (psychotherapy.org.uk).
- **Germany.** Therapie.de or the regional Kassenärztliche Vereinigung.
- **France.** Annuaire des psychologues via the relevant ordre.
- **Australia.** APS find-a-psychologist (psychology.org.au).
- **Other regions.** CM identifies the most credible national directory.

### Named PM-friendly clinicians (where verified by CM)

Add 1–3 clinicians the CM has personally confirmed are (a) licensed in the learner's region, (b) PM-friendly, and (c) currently taking referrals.

1. **[Clinician Name], [credential].** [Location] · [Languages] · [Specialty] · [Contact] · [PM familiarity note]
2. **[Clinician Name], [credential].** [Location] · [Languages] · [Specialty] · [Contact] · [PM familiarity note]
3. **[Clinician Name], [credential].** [Location] · [Languages] · [Specialty] · [Contact] · [PM familiarity note]

### Note for the learner (include in the packet)

> **PM coaching is not licensed therapy.** A PM-aware coach (Section 3) can hold thoughtware work with you — grounding, feelings work, drama identification, gremlin work. A licensed mental health professional is trained and regulated to work with diagnosable conditions, trauma, addiction, and severe distress.
>
> If you're working on patterns, choices, and how you show up — PM coaching fits. If you're working with diagnosable depression or anxiety, active trauma, addiction, suicidality, an eating disorder, or anything that needs medication management — that is a licensed clinician's domain. You can have both at the same time, and many learners do.

---

## 5. TRAUMA-QUALIFIED CLINICIANS (template)

> Include for any learner who routed through Screen 2 (Trauma & Dissociation) at intake — even if they passed without restrictions. Many CMs leave it in for all learners.

### The PM-precise distinction

PM distinguishes between two things that look similar from outside:

- **Emotional Healing Process (EHP)** — *in scope* for PM. Completing feelings that have been incomplete, often for a long time. EHP can be intense; it is held with grounding, partner support, and the consent practices in this course.
- **Trauma processing** — *not in scope* for PM. Reorganization of nervous-system patterning around overwhelming events. This is the work of a trauma-qualified clinician.

A learner with significant trauma who treats this course as a substitute for trauma care will likely get hurt. The course can be valuable for that learner — *alongside* a trauma-qualified clinician who is in the loop.

### What "trauma-qualified" means

Trained and currently practicing in one or more of:

- **EMDR** (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing)
- **Somatic Experiencing** (Levine)
- **Sensorimotor Psychotherapy** (Ogden)
- **TF-CBT** (Trauma-Focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy)
- **IFS** (Internal Family Systems, when used for trauma)
- **Equivalent evidence-based modality** the CM can verify

A generalist therapist who lists "trauma" as one of many interests is not trauma-qualified for this purpose. Look for the modality.

### Named trauma-qualified clinicians (where verified by CM)

1. **[Clinician Name], [credential].** [Location] · [Modality] · [Languages] · [Contact]
2. **[Clinician Name], [credential].** [Location] · [Modality] · [Languages] · [Contact]
3. **[Clinician Name], [credential].** [Location] · [Modality] · [Languages] · [Contact]

### Default — regional directories

If no named clinicians for the learner's region:

- **EMDR International Association** (emdria.org).
- **Somatic Experiencing International** (traumahealing.org).
- **IFS Institute** (ifs-institute.com).
- Regional equivalents (EMDR-Europe; EMDR UK & Ireland Association).

---

## 5A. ADDICTION & RECOVERY RESOURCES (template — serves Screen 3)

> Include for any learner who flagged **Screen 3 (Active addiction / compulsive behavior)** at intake (Doc 04 Section A.2). Screen 3 covers more than alcohol and drugs: it names **self-harm, restricting/bingeing food, gambling, sexual behavior, and screens/scrolling.** The resources below cover that full range. The national pointers are safe to keep in every packet; the disordered-eating and self-harm pointers especially belong in any packet where Screen 3 touched food or self-harm.
>
> Doc 04 Screen 3 defers or gates a flagged learner until there is **active professional support in writing** — a 12-step sponsor plus meeting attendance, or a treating clinician. This section is how the CM points the learner at that support. It is not a substitute for the licensed-clinician section (§4); for a diagnosable substance use or eating disorder, §4 applies in parallel.

### How to use this section

- Lead with the learner's **own country's** national line/service, then list the cross-border fallbacks.
- For a learner whose Screen 3 answer was about **food**, foreground the disordered-eating pointers. For **self-harm**, foreground the self-harm + crisis-line pointers (crisis lines live in §2 and are already first in the packet).
- Mutual-aid meetings (AA, NA, SMART) are free and immediate; clinical treatment (via §4 and the locators below) runs alongside, not instead.

### United States

- **SAMHSA National Helpline — 1-800-662-HELP (4357).** Free, confidential, 24/7, 365 days, English and Spanish. Treatment referral and information for substance use *and* mental-health concerns. Also text your ZIP code to **435748 (HELP4U)**. *(Number confirmed current at last review against samhsa.gov — re-confirm each quarter.)*
- **SAMHSA treatment locator — findtreatment.gov.** Searchable directory of licensed substance-use and mental-health treatment facilities. Pair it with the helpline number above rather than quoting a separate number from the locator page.
- **SMART Recovery — smartrecovery.org.** Free, evidence-based (CBT / motivational interviewing) mutual-aid meetings, in person and online. Covers any addictive or compulsive behavior — alcohol, drugs, gambling, and others — not just substances.
- **Alcoholics Anonymous — aa.org/find-aa.** Free meetings everywhere; in-person, online, and phone.
- **Narcotics Anonymous — na.org** (meeting search at na.org/meetingsearch). Free; open to anyone.

### Disordered eating (United States)

- **National Eating Disorders Association (NEDA) — nationaleatingdisorders.org/get-help.** Free, confidential screening tool, a specialist treatment finder, and low-cost peer-support options. **Note for the CM:** NEDA closed its telephone helpline; do not put a NEDA phone number in a packet. NEDA now routes urgent need to text support and screening. Confirm against their site each quarter.
- **Crisis Text Line — text NEDA to 741741.** 24/7 text support for someone in distress about eating or body concerns. (This is the same Crisis Text Line in §2, reached with the keyword **NEDA** instead of **HOME**.)
- **NAMI HelpLine — nami.org/help.** Information and referral for mental-health concerns including eating disorders. **Not a crisis line and not 24/7** (staffed business hours, weekdays); for after-hours or crisis use §2.
- For an active eating disorder, treat it as a clinical condition: use §4 (licensed clinicians) in parallel, and §2 crisis lines if there is acute risk.

### Self-harm (United States)

- **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline** (call or text **988**) and **Crisis Text Line** (text **HOME** to **741741**) — both already first in the packet (§2). For self-harm without suicidal intent these are still the right immediate contacts.
- For ongoing self-harm, a licensed clinician (§4), ideally trauma-qualified (§5) when self-harm is trauma-linked, is the standing support.

### United Kingdom

- **FRANK — talktofrank.com.** Honest drug and alcohol information and confidential advice. Phone **0300 123 6600** (24/7); text **82111**. *(Confirmed current at last review against talktofrank.com — re-confirm each quarter.)*
- **We Are With You (WithYou) — wearewithyou.org.uk.** Free, confidential drug, alcohol, and mental-health support, including a web chat with trained recovery workers seven days a week, and local services across England and Scotland.
- **SMART Recovery UK — smartrecovery.org.uk.** Free evidence-based meetings, online and in person.
- **Beat (eating disorders) — beateatingdisorders.org.uk.** The UK eating-disorder charity, with helplines and online support. The CM confirms the current helpline number from Beat's site before adding it to a packet rather than quoting a number from memory.

### Your region (fallback for any country not listed above)

> If your country is not listed above, search **"[your national health service] + addiction support"** or **"[your country] drug and alcohol helpline"** — for example, the NHS in the UK, your national or state health department, or your GP/family doctor. For an immediate crisis (including self-harm or suicidal thoughts), use the crisis lines at the top of this packet (§2) or **findahelpline.com**, which lists vetted lines by country. Mutual-aid fellowships are global: **AA (aa.org)**, **NA (na.org)**, and **SMART Recovery (smartrecovery.org)** all have meeting finders that cover most countries.

### CM note (do not include in the learner-facing packet)

- **Do not invent a helpline number.** Where a number is given above it was verified against the organization's own site at last review; re-confirm each quarter (Section 9). Where it is not given (NEDA, NAMI, Beat, the directories), name the organization and its site and have the learner — or you — confirm the current number, rather than guessing digits.
- Mutual aid is **not** clinical treatment and **not** a gate substitute. Doc 04 Screen 3 requires written confirmation of active support before access (sponsor + meetings, or a treating clinician). A list of meetings is a starting point, not the confirmation itself.
- For a learner with both addiction and trauma (common), §5 and §5A apply together; for both addiction and a mood/psychotic disorder, add §4 and, for Screen 5, the prescriber.

## 6. DOMESTIC VIOLENCE & LEGAL RESOURCES (regional, conditional)

> **Include only if Screen 4 returned yes at intake** (Doc 04 Section A.2). Otherwise remove this section.

### Crisis and support lines by region

- **United States.** National Domestic Violence Hotline — **1-800-799-7233** (call, text, chat at **thehotline.org**, 24/7). RAINN (sexual assault) — **1-800-656-HOPE (4673)**.
- **United Kingdom.** Refuge National Domestic Abuse Helpline — **0808 2000 247** (24/7). Galop (LGBT+ DV) — **0800 999 5428**.
- **Canada.** ShelterSafe (sheltersafe.ca) — shelter directory by province.
- **Australia.** 1800RESPECT — **1800 737 732** (24/7).
- **EU general.** WAVE (Women Against Violence Europe) — **wave-network.org**.
- **International.** Hot Peach Pages — **hotpeachpages.net**.

### Legal services note

Add for any learner whose Screen 4 answer involved rights, visa status, custody, or property at risk:

> If your situation involves immigration status, custody, housing, or financial rights, building safety often involves a lawyer in parallel with safety planning. Regional legal aid organizations can help even where private counsel isn't affordable. The CM will help identify the right starting point for your region.

CM identifies the legal-aid pointer for the learner's region — examples:

- **United States.** LawHelp.org — state-by-state legal aid.
- **United Kingdom.** Citizens Advice; Rights of Women.
- **Canada.** Provincial legal aid offices.
- **Australia.** Legal Aid in the learner's state.
- **EU.** Country-specific legal aid; FRA (EU Agency for Fundamental Rights) for cross-border.

### CM note (do not include in the learner-facing packet)

For any Screen-4 learner: confirm the act of receiving this packet does not put the learner at risk. If intake indicated devices or accounts may be monitored, deliver through a channel the learner has confirmed is private (separate email, physical print, encrypted messenger). The packet itself becomes the risk if delivered carelessly.

---

## 7. PM TRAINER PATH AND ONGOING COMMUNITY

> For learners who completed the course and want to deepen. Not crisis-side — opportunity-side. Include from the start so the learner knows what's beyond Day 10.

### Deepening pathways

- **PM Trainer Path.** The structured next-step training for learners who want to become PM practitioners. See the **PM main site at possibilitymanagement.org** for current details. (CM: update with the exact URL slug at quarterly review if known. Do not invent a URL.)
- **Possibility Lab.** Longer, immersive deepening containers — typically multi-week intensives. Run by senior PM trainers globally. See possibilitymanagement.org.
- **Possibility Team.** Small ongoing groups (often weekly) where PM is practiced live between trainings: dangerous questions, experiments, feedback. **Open to anyone** — no invitation and no prerequisite training; a learner can join an existing team or start one. See possibilitymanagement.org. Module 11's continuation material shows a course pairing how to become one.
- **Local PM Spark groups and Sparks salons.** Regular in-person or online gatherings where PM is practiced live. Often the most useful next step for a learner who wants community and practice without committing to a full training. The CM maintains a list of currently active Sparks and salons by region as part of the quarterly refresh.

### Reading

- **Conscious Feelings — Clinton Callahan.** The foundational PM text on the emotional body and feelings work. Available through possibilitymanagement.org and major book retailers.
- Other Callahan titles (e.g., *Radiant Joy Brilliant Love*, *Directing the Power of Conscious Feelings*) are referenced in specific module readings; the CM points learners to whichever fits where they want to go next.

### CM note

Some learners look for "what's next" by the end of Day 5. Others wait until after Day 10. Include this section in every packet from the start so a learner who is ready does not have to ask.

---

## 8. The not-therapy disclaimer (verbatim, in every learner's packet)

> Expand the Box is an educational course in Possibility Management. It is not therapy, counseling, trauma treatment, or psychiatric care. It does not substitute for any of these. If you are in mental health crisis, contact one of the crisis lines above immediately. If you are working through significant trauma, addiction, severe anxiety/depression, suicidality, mania, psychosis, or any condition that needs licensed care, please ensure that care is in place alongside (or in lieu of) the course. The community manager can pause your cohort access at any time so you can prioritize that care. The course will be here when you come back.

This paragraph appears in **every** packet, verbatim. Do not paraphrase. Do not soften. Do not move it to a footnote. It belongs in the body, after the crisis lines and before the coach/clinician lists.

---

## 9. CM maintenance notes

Reviewed and refreshed by the CM at least **quarterly** and at the start of every new cohort.

### Quarterly checklist

- [ ] **Crisis lines verified.** Open each national directory; confirm every number, text shortcode, and web address. Numbers change (988 replaced the US lifeline only in 2022; Canada's 9-8-8 launched in 2023).
- [ ] **PM-aware coaches confirmed.** Email or call each coach in Section 3. Remove those not practicing or not accepting clients. Add new ones as the directory grows.
- [ ] **Licensed clinicians confirmed.** Same for Section 4.
- [ ] **Trauma-qualified clinicians confirmed.** Same for Section 5. Track newly trained or newly visible practitioners.
- [ ] **Addiction & recovery resources verified.** Confirm Section 5A. Re-check the SAMHSA number (1-800-662-HELP) against samhsa.gov and the FRANK number (0300 123 6600) against talktofrank.com; confirm NEDA still has no phone helpline before relying on the text/screening routing; verify Beat's current helpline number from its site before quoting it. Numbers in this space change and helplines close (NEDA's phone line closed in 2023) — assume nothing.
- [ ] **DV and legal resources verified.** Confirm Section 6 numbers and URLs.
- [ ] **New countries added.** If recent cohorts brought learners from countries not yet templated, add their crisis line, clinician directory, and (if relevant) DV resource.
- [ ] **PM site URLs.** Update Section 7 pointers if possibilitymanagement.org has changed structure.
- [ ] **Date the review.** Below.

### Per-cohort check (in addition to quarterly)

- [ ] Generate the packet from this template for each enrolled learner using their intake data.
- [ ] For any Screen-4 learner, confirm the delivery channel is private.
- [ ] Confirm packet delivery before content access opens (Doc 04 Section K).

## 10. Worked exemplar — Referral Packet — United States

> **What this is.** A fully-worked example of the per-learner packet, generated from this template for a hypothetical US learner who flagged Screens 2, 3, and 4 at intake. It proves the template produces a complete, real-resource packet end-to-end. It is **not** a learner record — it is a reference the CM copies and adapts. Every resource below is real; the only blanks are the PM-aware coach slots, which genuinely require the operator (see the note at the end).
>
> **How it was built.** Country = United States. Screen 1 none. Screen 2 yes (so §5 trauma clinicians included). Screen 3 yes — alcohol (so §5A included). Screen 4 yes (so §6 DV/legal included). Screen 5 none. Sections appear in packet order: crisis lines first, disclaimer, then support lists.

---

### Crisis lines (always first)

- **United States.** **988** — Suicide and Crisis Lifeline (call or text). Crisis Text Line — text **HOME** to **741741**.
- **International directory.** **findahelpline.com** — if you are travelling or your number above does not connect.

> **If you are in immediate physical danger to yourself or another person right now, contact 988 above OR call 911. The course is paused while you are safe.**

> If your country is not listed above, see **findahelpline.com** or search **"[your country] suicide crisis line"** immediately. Do not delay because the list doesn't have your number — the directories exist precisely for this.

### The not-therapy disclaimer

> Expand the Box is an educational course in Possibility Management. It is not therapy, counseling, trauma treatment, or psychiatric care. It does not substitute for any of these. If you are in mental health crisis, contact one of the crisis lines above immediately. If you are working through significant trauma, addiction, severe anxiety/depression, suicidality, mania, psychosis, or any condition that needs licensed care, please ensure that care is in place alongside (or in lieu of) the course. The community manager can pause your cohort access at any time so you can prioritize that care. The course will be here when you come back.

### Licensed mental health professionals (United States)

> **PM coaching is not licensed therapy.** A PM-aware coach can hold thoughtware work with you — grounding, feelings work, drama identification, gremlin work. A licensed mental health professional is trained and regulated to work with diagnosable conditions, trauma, addiction, and severe distress. You can have both at the same time, and many learners do.

- **Psychology Today — psychologytoday.com.** Largest US directory; filter by location, insurance, specialty, and modality.
- **Therapy Den — therapyden.com.** Values-aligned filtering (identity, approach, sliding scale).
- *(CM adds 1–2 personally-verified PM-friendly clinicians here when available — confirm licensed in the learner's state, PM-friendly, and currently taking referrals.)*

### Trauma-qualified clinicians (Screen 2 applied)

> PM distinguishes **Emotional Healing Process** (completing incomplete feelings — *in scope*) from **trauma processing** (reorganizing nervous-system patterning around overwhelming events — *out of scope*). The course can be valuable alongside a trauma-qualified clinician who is in the loop — not as a substitute. Look for the modality, not a generalist who lists "trauma" among many interests.

- **EMDR International Association — emdria.org** ("Find an EMDR Therapist" directory). EMDR-trained, US-wide.
- **Somatic Experiencing International — traumahealing.org** (practitioner directory).
- **IFS Institute — ifs-institute.com** (find-a-practitioner, when IFS is used for trauma).

### Addiction & recovery (Screen 3 applied — alcohol)

- **SAMHSA National Helpline — 1-800-662-HELP (4357).** Free, confidential, 24/7, English and Spanish. Treatment referral and information. Text your ZIP to **435748 (HELP4U)**.
- **SAMHSA treatment locator — findtreatment.gov.** Licensed substance-use and mental-health treatment facilities near you.
- **SMART Recovery — smartrecovery.org.** Free evidence-based meetings, online and in person.
- **Alcoholics Anonymous — aa.org/find-aa.** Free meetings; in person, online, and phone.
- **Narcotics Anonymous — na.org.** Free; open to anyone (na.org/meetingsearch).
- *(If your Screen 3 concern is food: NEDA — nationaleatingdisorders.org/get-help — free screening and a treatment finder; for distress in the moment, text **NEDA** to **741741**. If it is self-harm: use **988** / Crisis Text Line above.)*

### Domestic violence & legal resources (Screen 4 applied)

- **National Domestic Violence Hotline — 1-800-799-7233.** Call, text, or chat at **thehotline.org**, 24/7.
- **RAINN (sexual assault) — 1-800-656-HOPE (4673).** 24/7; chat at rainn.org.
- **LawHelp.org — lawhelp.org.** State-by-state legal aid for custody, housing, immigration, and financial rights.

> If your situation involves immigration status, custody, housing, or financial rights, building safety often involves a lawyer in parallel with safety planning. Regional legal aid organizations can help even where private counsel isn't affordable. The CM will help identify the right starting point.

*(CM note, not learner-facing: this learner flagged Screen 4 — confirm the delivery channel is private before sending, per §6 and Doc 04 A.2.)*

### PM-aware coaches (operator fills)

- **[operator fills from the PM Trainer Path graduate directory at possibilitymanagement.org]** — US-based, English-speaking, currently practicing and accepting clients, rate confirmed for the learner's region. See §3 for the field template and the confirm-before-shipping checklist.

### PM Trainer Path and ongoing community

- **PM Trainer Path / Possibility Lab / Sparks** — see **possibilitymanagement.org** for current details. **Conscious Feelings (Clinton Callahan)** is the foundational PM text on feelings work.

> **End of exemplar.** The only blanks above are the PM-aware coach slots, which require the operator's directory and live confirmation. Everything else is a real, current resource.

---

> **End of template.** This document is the source. Per-learner packets are generated from it. When in doubt about scope, voice, or structure, return to the source rather than improvising — and update the source if the improvisation is generally useful.
