---

# Reflection and Journal Prompt Bank

> A library of prompts the learner pulls from when the module's prompts have been used up — or when the module's prompts went somewhere the learner is not yet ready to go and a side-door is needed.

---

## How to use this file

Each Day-module gives you 4–5 reflection prompts in its own file. Those are the primary set. This bank is the secondary set — prompts to reach for when you want to go deeper, when you want a side angle, when you have a week to integrate and the module's prompts have all been worked.

There are eight sections. Day-by-day deeper prompts (Days 1–10). Cross-module integration prompts. Weekly review prompts. End-of-cohort prompts. The dangerous questions list. Prompts for when you are stuck.

**Pick one. Sit with it for at least 20 minutes before writing.** Journaling without sitting first produces the same thoughts you have already had on paper. Sitting first lets the question work on you. Then write.

**Longhand if you can.** The body is in the writing when it is in the hand. Typing is faster and lands less.

---

## The difference between a journal prompt and a self-help question

This is worth naming once.

A **self-help question** asks you to perform what you already know about yourself. *"What are your top three values?" "What are you grateful for today?" "What would your best self do?"* These produce predictable answers because the answer is constructed by the part of you the question addresses — usually your Adapted Child performing wellness, or your Nurturing Parent performing self-care.

A **PM-grade journal prompt** does something different. It is engineered to bypass the Box's prepared answers and land in a body location where you have not been looking. The form of a good prompt is: *not what you would say about yourself in a job interview, but what your body knows that your story has not let you say.*

Two tests:
1. **Does the question dissolve the floor a little?** If after asking it you feel slightly less certain rather than more, you are likely in the right territory.
2. **Did you already know the answer?** If yes, the question is performing — it is not yet PM-grade. Push to the version of the question whose answer you do not already have.

This file is full of the second kind. Some of them will be uncomfortable. That is the point.

---

## Day-by-day deeper prompts

Beyond what each module's own prompts cover. Reach for these in the integration period of each Day-cycle.

### Day 1 — Orientation, New Context, Radical Responsibility

1. The context I told my partner I am operating from when I recorded my first voice message, versus the context I am actually operating from today — what is the gap, and what is in the gap? (The gap is rarely zero. The gap is often where the work is.)
2. When I read the four levels of responsibility — child, adolescent, adult, radical — which one am I most fluent at *performing* without actually inhabiting? When I look back at my last seven days, where did I look like the level I am claiming, and where was I actually one level lower?
3. The red-pill sentence I chose — three weeks from now, having now seen what the course is, would I choose it differently? More cleanly? More honestly? Less performatively? What would the actual cleanly-chosen version say?
4. Whose authority have I been treating as mine, in the area of life this course is meant to address? Whose voice has been making my decisions and getting credit?
5. If I knew with certainty that the next 30 days of this course would change my life in a specific direction — what direction would I be most afraid it changed in?
6. What did the people who matter to me — partner, children, family, close friends — do when I announced (or did not announce) that I was taking this course? What did their reaction tell me about the container I am embedded in?

### Day 2 — Thoughtware, Thoughtmaps, Box Technology

1. Looking at the list of Box rules I cataloged, which rule is the load-bearing one — the one that, if removed, several others would fall away too? Why has it been load-bearing for me, specifically?
2. Whose Box did I install? Was it one parent, both, a teacher, a religion, an early painful event? When I name the source precisely, does the rule feel different?
3. There is a Box rule I have not written down because it would be too embarrassing or too revealing. Without writing it here either, can I name to myself that it exists? Where in my body do I notice it living?
4. If I let my Box continue running for the next 10 years exactly as it is now — what would my life look like at the end of those 10 years? Where would I be? What would have happened? What would not have happened?
5. Which Box rule does my partner (life-partner, not pairing-partner) most rely on me running? What would happen between us if I stopped running it?
6. The Box says *you are the Box*. What is the part of me that disagrees with that, and where does that part live in my body?

### Day 3 — Liquid State, Center-Ground-Bubble, Five Bodies

1. Of the five bodies, which one have I been treating as my whole self — and which one have I been treating as if it did not exist? What does the second one know about my life that I have been refusing to hear?
2. When I am off-center — handing my authority to someone else's reaction or yesterday's conversation or my phone — what is the consistent flavor of where my center keeps going? Whose center am I borrowing? Whose problem am I trying to be on top of?
3. The first time I dropped a grounding cord during a real conversation — what was different in the conversation, and what was different in me afterwards? If I had been grounded my whole life this way, what would my life look like now?
4. My bubble — historically too big, too small, too porous, or too rigid? What did the size do for me protectively, and what is it costing me now?
5. The golden cube was for placing work inside and looking at. What is one thing in my life right now that, if I placed it in the cube and looked at it from outside — without solving — would shift its weight?
6. The Box performs stillness. The Adult is actually still. What is the difference, in my body, between the two?

### Day 4 — Feedback, Coaching, Rapid Learning, Experiments

1. The last time someone offered me PM-grade feedback I could have used — what did I do with it? Did I rebut, redirect, deflect, or take it in? What does that pattern tell me about my Box's relationship to being seen accurately?
2. The Beeps I have caught in the last week — what is the pattern? Which Beep keeps repeating? What is the upstream thoughtware that keeps producing the same Beep?
3. There is a piece of feedback I have been receiving from multiple people for years that I have not taken in. What is it? What would it cost me to take it in fully? What has refusing to take it in cost me?
4. In which area of my life have I been parked at *"I read about it"* for years — meaning: I have the knowledge, I have not run the experiments? What is one experiment I could run this week?
5. The most generous coach I could have for the next 90 days — what kind of person would they be? Why am I not yet working with them?
6. PM coaching offers possibilities, not answers. The last time I asked someone for advice — was I asking for possibilities or trying to be told the answer? What would have happened if I had been given possibilities and asked to choose?

### Day 5 — Feelings vs Emotions, Old Map of Feelings, Numbness Bar

1. Of the four feelings, which one has the highest Numbness Bar over it for me? Specifically: which feeling, if it fully registered, would change my life most? Which is the Numbness Bar protecting me from?
2. The Old Map I came in with — who installed which rule? Map each of the four feelings to the specific person, institution or event that gave me the rule. The map is more precise than I expect when actually drawn.
3. The 5-minute test on my own current internal state: which "feelings" in my daily life are actually emotions, looping past the 5-minute mark, posing as present-time information about my present-time life?
4. The feeling I am most uncomfortable feeling at full intensity — what would I do with the information that feeling carries, if I let it land? Whose life would change?
5. There is a feeling that has been waiting under my numbness for years, asking to register. If I let 5% of it through this week, what would surface? What am I afraid that 5% would lead to?
6. My partner (life-partner, family member, child) — which of their feelings does my Numbness Bar prevent me from registering? Whose grief have I been not seeing? Whose anger have I been minimizing? What costs has that had to them, and to us?

### Day 6 — Mixed Emotions and Emotional Healing Process

1. The stored emotion I carry that I have most thoroughly disguised as personality — what is it actually? What feelings make up the mix? When did it start being mistaken for "who I am"?
2. The emotional wound that has not closed in me, despite years of trying — what conditions does it actually need to close, that I have not yet provided? What container, what witness, what time?
3. The trigger I keep falling for — the small thing in the present that activates the large emotion from the past. What is the trigger, what is the source, and what would change if I addressed the source rather than the trigger?
4. Which of my current important relationships is being run not by the present-day person but by the emotional charge from someone earlier? Whose ghost am I in conversation with when I am supposedly in conversation with this person?
5. The grief I have not yet allowed myself to feel — for the person, the relationship, the dream, the version of myself, the chapter — what would change if I gave it the full 3–7 days it actually needs?
6. There is an emotional healing process waiting, but not yet ready. What would have to be in place — what container, what witness, what professional support, what time of life — for me to do it cleanly? When?

### Day 7 — Low Drama, Gremlin Food, Shifting to Responsible Game

1. The gremlin food I am most addicted to — the one I would defend even now, even after this module — what is it? What does it provide that I do not yet know how to provide cleanly?
2. The drama position I run with my partner — Persecutor, Victim, Rescuer — and the drama position they run with me: how does our rotation work? Who initiates? Who follows? What gets produced?
3. There is a fight I keep having in my life. Same shape, different content. What is the shape? What gremlin food does it produce reliably? What would happen if I declined the fight one day and noticed what tries to take its place?
4. The Responsible Game move scares me because — what specifically? What am I afraid would be revealed if I dropped the drama and stood in what is actually here?
5. My favorite rescue — the help I deliver to others that they did not ask for, that allows me not to do my own work — who do I deliver it to most reliably? What does the rescue cost them in autonomy, and what does it cost me in honesty?
6. The narrative I tell about my hardest year (or relationship, or chapter) — how much of the narrative is true storytelling, and how much is gremlin food production? If I subtracted the gremlin food, what would the remaining story be?

### Day 8 — Listening, Speaking, Communication, Completion Loops

1. The conversation I have been not having for the longest time — with whom, about what, and what specifically is the unfinished piece? What would happen if I ran the completion loop this week?
2. Of the 12 Roadblocks, which is my go-to under pressure? When did I learn to deploy it? Who taught me — by example or by absence?
3. The person in my life I almost never offer Possibility Listening to — who is it, and what would change if I held that vacuum space for them once? What am I afraid would emerge?
4. Speaking from story versus sourcing — when I review my speech this week, what percentage came from each? When I sourced, what was different in the room?
5. The truth I have been trying to say for months, in many partial versions, and which is not yet landing — what is the one true sentence underneath all the partial versions? What does my body do when I imagine saying it?
6. Who in my life listens to me at the level I am capable of speaking from? Where is the gap between the speech I can produce and the listening available — and what does that gap make me do (over-explain, perform, withdraw, soften)?

### Day 9 — Ego States, Problem Ownership, Learning Spiral

1. My default ego state — the one I run most of my life from — what is it actually, when I am honest? (Common Box-trap: claiming Adult while running Critical Parent. Claiming Free Child while running Adapted Child. Claiming insight while running Gremlin.)
2. My Critical Parent's voice belongs to whom, originally? Trace the voice. What did it once protect me from? What does it now cost me?
3. My Adapted Child runs more of my adult life than I would like to admit. What does it most reliably perform? Pleasing? Hiding? Rebelling? Comply-then-resent? Whom does it perform for?
4. The problem I have been carrying that is not actually mine — whose is it? What did I gain by carrying it? What would change for them — and for me — if I gave it back?
5. My Demon — the part of me with the capacity for real harm — what is its specific capacity? Where, in my life, has it acted? What conditions reduce the likelihood of it acting again?
6. The learning spiral — the same lesson keeps coming back at deeper levels of the same territory. Which lesson is currently on its third or fourth pass through my life? What is the level underneath the one I have already worked?

### Day 10 — Map of Possibility, Bright Principles, Three Powers, Integration

1. The bright principle I am most willing to source from — the one I can name and feel? And the bright principle that has been quietly available but I have not yet picked up — what is it, and why have I not?
2. The shadow principle I have most quietly been sourcing from, throughout my adult life — what is it? What has it given me? What has it cost?
3. The declaration I am ready to make, but have not yet made — what is it? What is the cost of not making it? What is the cost of making it?
4. The dangerous question I have been carrying without asking — what is it? When will I ask it? What am I waiting for?
5. The three powers — choosing, asking dangerous questions, declaring. Which of the three is most underdeveloped in me? Which would change my life most if I practiced it for 90 days?
6. The version of me 90 days from today who has practiced these powers consistently — what is they doing that I am not doing today? What did they start doing on Day 1 after the course closed?

---

## Cross-module integration prompts

These connect distinctions across modules. The most generative integration work happens at the intersections.

1. Which feeling is most numbed for me, and which ego state covers for it? (Common pattern: Critical Parent covers numbed anger. Nurturing Parent covers numbed sadness. Gremlin covers numbed joy. Adapted Child covers numbed fear.) When I see the covering, what becomes available?
2. Where in my life is the Box still running, even though I now see it? Why has seeing it not yet stopped it? What does the Box still provide that I have not yet built another way to provide?
3. Which gremlin food am I most attached to, and what declaration would starve it? (The declaration that makes the gremlin food no longer available is the one worth making.)
4. The Old Map of Feelings I inherited, and the drama positions I run — how are they linked? Forbidden anger usually produces Victim or Rescuer; forbidden sadness usually produces Persecutor; forbidden fear usually produces all three at different times.
5. My default ego state and my default drama position — which combinations consistently appear together? (Critical Parent + Persecutor. Adapted Child + Victim. Nurturing Parent + Rescuer.) What thoughtware would interrupt the combination?
6. The Box rules I cataloged on Day 2, and the listening roadblocks I deploy on Day 8 — how are they related? Each roadblock is a Box rule's strategy for staying intact while seeming to listen.
7. The feeling I most struggle to register, and the bright principle I most struggle to source from — are they linked? Numbed joy and unsourced gratitude. Numbed anger and unsourced courage. Numbed sadness and unsourced honesty. Numbed fear and unsourced presence.
8. The conversation I most need to complete, and the ego state I would have to be in to complete it cleanly — are they aligned? Most uncompleted conversations are waiting for the Adult, while I keep showing up as Adapted Child or Critical Parent.
9. The dangerous question I am avoiding, and the decision I am stuck on — are they the same question in different costume? Most stuck decisions are stuck because the question underneath has not been asked.
10. What I learned on Day 5 about emotions and what I learned on Day 9 about problem ownership — how do they meet? A misowned problem is often a misidentified emotion. Whose anger have I been carrying? Whose grief? Whose fear?

---

## Weekly review prompts

End of each cohort week. 15–20 minutes. Reach for these in addition to (not instead of) the modules' own reflection prompts.

1. **What landed this week.** Not what was interesting, not what I knew already, not what I performed knowing. What landed — meaning: I am living slightly differently because of it. One sentence.
2. **What did not land yet.** Where the module did its work but the work has not yet integrated. The territory I am still working on. One sentence.
3. **What I avoided.** One thing the module asked me to do or look at that I did not do or look at. Why I did not. Without making it into a fresh task.
4. **The Box's strategy this week.** What did the Box do specifically to keep itself intact through this week's material? Which strategy did it favor — disengagement, intellectualization, sycophancy with the course, performative compliance, busy-ness, distraction?
5. **The pairing this week.** What was alive in my partner exchange that I have not yet said back to them? What did I notice about their work that I did not yet reflect? One thing I will say in the next exchange.
6. **The single most useful question I asked myself this week.** Why was it useful? Where can I ask it again in a different territory?

---

## End-of-cohort prompts (after Day 10)

These are substantial. Set aside an hour or two. These are the prompts that anchor the 90-day post-course period.

1. **The change of context.** What is the context I am now operating from in the area of my life that mattered most going in? Is it different from when I started? Where can I still feel the old context trying to reassert itself, and what does it sound like?
2. **The Box now.** What does my Box look like now that I have seen it for 30 days? What has shifted? What is unchanged? Which of its rules am I still living inside, and which have softened? Which new ones have appeared that were not visible 30 days ago?
3. **The cost of completing this course.** Every thoughtware upgrade has a cost — what something was paying for becomes more visible once that something is gone. What has it cost me to complete this course? Who in my life has paid for it alongside me? Who has not paid and is in a different position than they would have been if they had?
4. **What the course was not.** Where did the course not go, that I needed it to go? What did I bring in that I am leaving still unaddressed? Where will I take that material next — therapy, coaching, longer PM work, a different course, a relationship reset?
5. **The version of me that walked in.** When I write the introduction-letter to my pairing partner from memory now, who was that person? What had they been doing for years that made the course necessary? What were they unable to say or feel or do?
6. **The version of me walking out.** Who is this person? What can they do that the version walking in could not? Where are they still tender? What practice keeps them from collapsing back into the old version?
7. **What I will declare for the 90 days.** One specific declaration. Specific. Public to one person. Reviewed weekly. What is it?
8. **The PM-grade community I want.** Who in my life — pairing partner, course alumni, PM-aware coaches, eventual possibility team members — am I committing to staying in PM-grade contact with? Specifically — names, cadence, container.
9. **The first crisis ahead.** I can predict, roughly, the next crisis-shape that will come in my life — work, relationship, family, health, identity. When it comes, what from this course will I most need to remember? Where will I most likely forget it? What will I write to myself now to read then?
10. **What the next 10 modules would be.** If I designed Days 11–20 of my own continuation course — what would they cover? What territory am I newly able to enter that I could not have entered before Day 1? What is the next edge?
11. **The dangerous question I am ready to ask now.** Which dangerous question, of all those that surfaced across the 10 modules, am I now ready to ask cleanly? When will I sit with it?
12. **What I am taking with me.** Not what I learned. What I am taking. One distinction, one practice, one relationship, one declaration. Four things. No more. The four things that, if everything else from the course dissolved, would still embed.

---

## The dangerous questions list

PM-canonical. Each of these dissolves a floor when sat with for at least 20 minutes. Pick one at a time. Do not ask them all in one sitting. Do not ask them in liquid state without equipment. Do not ask them when alone if a partner-message check-in is not available afterwards.

1. What am I pretending not to know?
2. Who would I be if I dropped this story about myself?
3. What would I have to be willing to feel in order to leave this?
4. What am I getting out of this not changing?
5. Whose approval am I actually waiting for? What would happen if I stopped?
6. What am I afraid would happen if I were fully seen — and is that fear current, or is it from another time?
7. What is the truth I have been not saying, to whom, and what is its cost so far?
8. If I knew I had two years left to live, which of my current commitments would I keep, and which would I drop within the week?
9. What did I want, as a child, that I have been not letting myself want as an adult?
10. Where am I most counterfeit? Where am I performing a version of myself that no one would miss if I dropped it?
11. Whose problem have I been making mine in order to avoid my own?
12. What am I building right now that I would not build if I were sourcing from joy instead of from fear?
13. What did I once know clearly about my own direction, that I have let other people's voices outshout?
14. The thing in me that is broken or wrong — what if it is neither broken nor wrong, just unfinished?
15. What is the most generous version of the person I am most in conflict with — and what does seeing them that way ask of me?

---

## Prompts for when you are stuck

For the learner who is stalling between modules. Read the prompts in order. Sit with whichever lands first. Do not skip; do not power through.

1. **The stall is information.** What is it information about? Not "I am being lazy" — that is the Box's verdict, not the data. What is the actual reason I am not picking up the next module? What does my body know about the next module's content that I have not yet let my mind know?
2. **The cost of continuing right now.** What would it cost me, today, to do the next module? Time? Energy? Disruption to a relationship? Activation of a feeling? Confrontation with a story I have been holding? Name the actual cost. The Box conceals the cost; naming it returns choice.
3. **The cost of not continuing.** What does it cost me, today, to not do the next module? What is the chain that begins with "I am going to stall this week" — what does that lead to, in 30 days, in 90 days? Is the cost of stalling actually smaller than the cost of doing?
4. **The pause that is actually a stop.** Sometimes the stall is the right pause — the system has been working hard and needs time. Sometimes the stall is the Box's stop dressed as a pause. Which is mine? The tell: a real pause has a specific re-entry date and a clear reason. A Box stop has neither.
5. **What I would do if I did not have to do it perfectly.** If the next module did not have to be done well — if I just had to read the file, run the practice imperfectly, send one voice message that was not impressive — could I do that today? Often the stall is perfectionism wearing the costume of integrity.
6. **Reach for the partner.** Have I told my pairing partner that I am stalling, and asked them to witness it? Have I told the CM? The stall, named to another human, almost always changes shape. The stall in private, however, can extend indefinitely.

---

## Final note

This file contains roughly 95 prompts. You will not use most of them in any single course cycle. They are here so that when you reach the end of the module's own prompts and want more — or when a particular question has been circling and you want a precise version of it — there is a place to go.

Three rules:

1. **Sit before writing.** 20 minutes minimum, in silence, with the prompt held in mind. Skipping the sit lands you in your standard answers.
2. **Longhand if you can.** The body is in the writing when it is in the hand.
3. **The question whose answer you don't already know is the right question.** If you already know the answer, push to the question whose answer you do not yet have.

Welcome to your inventory.

---

---

# Solo Centering and Grounding Scripts

> A library of script-ready solo practices for the learner. Read once. Then use.

---

## When to use this file

This file is for you, the learner. It contains eleven scripts. Some are 60 seconds — for when you need to come back into your body in the middle of a module and decide what to do next. Some are 12 minutes — the standard energetic-body practice you will run before every High module from Day 5 onwards. Some are emergency scripts for the specific things that happen: crying that won't stop, going numb, the disorientation of liquid state, the over-stimulated nervous system at the end of a processing day.

None of these are meditations. They are PM practices. They install distinctions through the body — center, ground, bubble, cube, the four feelings as energies, the difference between feeling and dissociating — and they are most useful when run as scripts you read aloud (or record once and play back) rather than as ideas you "do in your head." Doing them in your head is the Box's performance of doing them.

**How to use this file:**
- Bookmark it. You will reach for it more in Weeks 2–4 of the course than in Week 1.
- Record the ones you use most as voice memos on your phone. Your own voice reading these to you is more useful than reading silently mid-state.
- Run them as written before you customize. Run as written for 30 days. Then adapt if you must.
- Share scripts 2 and 7–10 with your pairing partner — those are the ones a partner may need to talk you through over voice message if you are flooded.
- These scripts do not replace the safety pathway in Doc 03. If you are in crisis, ground first, then reach the buddy and the CM. The scripts are the *first move*, not the only move.

The 60-second universal grounding script is reproduced from Doc 03 Section D for use anywhere — including for sharing on the cohort feed when you want to ask the cohort to ground with you. The other ten scripts are new and live only here.

---

## 1. The 60-second universal grounding script

This is the script. The one at the top of every module. The default action whenever you notice you have gone far away from yourself.

> **Grounding (60 seconds).**
>
> 1. Stop whatever you were doing in the module. Close the video or the document.
> 2. Stand up. Both feet on the floor. Knees soft.
> 3. Breathe out longer than you breathe in, three times. Audible exhale is fine.
> 4. Name three things you can see in the room.
> 5. Name one thing you can hear.
> 6. Place one hand on your sternum, the other on your belly. Notice that your body is here.
> 7. When you have come back, decide: continue, pause for 10 minutes, or stop the module for today.

Use this any time you notice:
- Feeling far away from yourself
- Inability to follow the words on the page or in the video
- Heart racing without clear cause
- Watching yourself from outside
- Crying that does not feel clean — feels stuck
- Strong urge to flee, attack, or shut down

These are nervous-system signals, not signs something is wrong with you. Ground first. Decide second.

---

## 2. The 5-minute centering practice

For the start of every module. Run it before you open the file. It is the difference between the module landing in your body and the module landing in your head as content.

> **Script. 5 minutes. Read aloud or play back. Eyes open or closed — your choice.**
>
> **Minute 1 — arrive.** Sit. Both feet on the floor. Spine upright but not braced. Shoulders down. Hands resting on your thighs or in your lap.
>
> Three breaths. Each exhale longer than the inhale. Make the exhale audible on at least one.
>
> Notice the chair under you. Notice the floor under your feet. Notice the temperature of the air on your face.
>
> **Minute 2 — find your center.** Move your attention to your lower belly — a hand's-width below your navel, behind the abdominal wall. This is the hara. For most people, this is where your center actually lives.
>
> Say out loud, or in your head if you need to: *"My center is here."*
>
> If your attention drifts to your head — to thoughts, to planning, to the day — notice. Bring it back to your belly. The drift is not a problem. The noticing-and-returning is the practice.
>
> If your center is not in your belly — if it actually lives behind your sternum, in your heart — locate it there. Where it actually is.
>
> **Minute 3 — drop a grounding cord.** From your center, declare a thick energetic cord — ship's-rope diameter — going down through your seat, through the floor, through the building, into the earth, all the way to the center.
>
> Say out loud: *"My grounding cord is dropped."*
>
> Not visualization. Declaration. It exists because you said so.
>
> Let excess energy drain. Tension from your day. Charge from the conversation you just had. The buzz of the screens. Allow. Do not push.
>
> **Minute 4 — set a bubble.** Declare a sphere of energy around you. Arm's length in every direction. Under your feet. Above your head.
>
> Say out loud: *"My bubble is set."*
>
> Inside the bubble: your body, your breath, your feelings, your rhythm, this module. Outside the bubble: the room beyond arm's length, the street noise, the partner you were thinking about, the email you have not answered. Real, *not yours, not right now*.
>
> If the bubble feels too big — pull it in. Too small or collapsed — push it out. Your choice.
>
> **Minute 5 — hold all three and arrive.** Center. Grounding cord. Bubble.
>
> Three breaths. Slow. Audible exhale.
>
> Now, out loud: *"I am here. I am centered. I am grounded. I am bubbled. I am ready to begin the module."*
>
> Open the document. Begin.

Run this before every module. Every single one. Including the Low intensity ones. By Module 4 it will take you 90 seconds because the equipment is yours; until then, run all five minutes.

---

## 3. Center + grounding cord + bubble — the standard practice (10 minutes)

This is the longer-form version of Practice 2. Run it once a day during the course if you can, and always before any High module (5, 6, 7, 9) — that is what the consent check is referring to when it says *"I know how to ground."*

> **Script. 10 minutes. Chair, water, no phone, no music. Read it through once, then run it.**
>
> **Minute 1 — settle.** Sit. Both feet on the floor. Drink water. Three audible exhales.
>
> Notice three things you can see. One thing you can hear. The temperature of your hands.
>
> **Minutes 2–3 — find center.** Move attention to the lower belly. Hand's-width below the navel, behind the abdominal wall.
>
> *"My center is here."*
>
> Stay there. Attention will drift up — to head, to thought, to plan. Each time you notice the drift, return. The rep is *noticing and returning*, not staying.
>
> If your center actually lives in your heart (some people, particularly artists and healers, do live there), locate it where it actually is. Trust where your body says it is.
>
> Place one hand on your center. Feel the rise and fall of breath through the hand. Sixty seconds.
>
> **Minutes 4–5 — drop the grounding cord.** From your center, declare a thick cord. Ship's-rope diameter. Down through your seat, through the floor, through whatever is beneath the floor, into the earth, into the bedrock, all the way to the core of the planet.
>
> *"My grounding cord is dropped."*
>
> Notice if your cord wants to be a different size or material. Some people's cord is a tree trunk; some, a column of light; some, a steel cable. Do not pick. Notice what it actually is.
>
> Let excess discharge. Energy that is not yours — what you absorbed from the last conversation, the news, the city — drains down the cord. Do not push it. Allow.
>
> Notice colour, weight, temperature in the cord. Do not interpret. Just note.
>
> **Minutes 6–7 — set the bubble.** Declare a sphere of energy around your body. Arm's length in every direction. Under your feet, above your head, behind you.
>
> *"My bubble is set."*
>
> Walk the perimeter of the bubble with your attention. Right. Behind. Left. Front. Above. Below. The edges are where your body's "yours" meets the world's "not yours."
>
> Notice the size. Too big — your energy is stretched thin, you are leaking into the room. Pull it in. Too small or collapsed — you are squeezed, you have given your space away to whoever or whatever is in your head. Push it out to arm's length.
>
> Inside: body, breath, feelings, rhythm, this practice. Outside: real, *not yours*.
>
> **Minutes 8–9 — hold all three.** Center. Cord. Bubble.
>
> Three slow breaths.
>
> Body scan, fast. Top of head — present? Throat — present? Heart — present? Hands — present? Belly — present? Pelvis — present? Legs — present? Feet — present? Note where you are clearly here and where you are still partly somewhere else. Do not fix it. Notice.
>
> **Minute 10 — choose.** What is next?
>
> If you are entering a module: *"I am centered, grounded, bubbled. I am beginning the module."*
>
> If you are entering a conversation: *"I am centered, grounded, bubbled. I am entering this conversation."*
>
> If you are entering sleep: *"I am centered, grounded, bubbled. I am letting the day complete."*
>
> Drink water. Stand up. Go.

The equipment compounds. By the third or fourth pass it is yours and you can run it in 60 seconds when you need to. Until then — full ten minutes. The reps build the perceptual interface.

---

## 4. The golden cube — workspace setup (12 minutes)

Run this when you are about to declare something or run a specific experiment in your actual life that the module surfaced. The golden cube is the energetic workspace inside which you place one piece of thoughtware work — a distinction to land, a piece of your Box to look at, a declaration to test, an experiment to design.

Not for daily use. For when there is something to construct or test.

> **Script. 12 minutes. Chair, water, notebook open. Read aloud or record.**
>
> **Minutes 1–4 — center, ground, bubble.** Run the practice from Script 3, shortened. Center. Cord. Bubble. Three breaths.
>
> Confirm: *"I am centered. I am grounded. I am bubbled."*
>
> Do not open the cube until the three are stable. If they are not stable, run Script 3 in full first.
>
> **Minute 5 — open the cube.** Two paces in front of you, declare a cube of golden light. Size of a small room — large enough to walk into if you wanted to, though you will not. Edges crisp. The interior is a workspace.
>
> *"The cube is open."*
>
> Notice the cube. Notice that it is two paces away, not on top of you, not inside you. The distance is the point. You are looking at it. You are not in it.
>
> **Minutes 6–7 — place the work inside.** Decide what you are placing inside the cube. **One piece only.** Common pieces:
> - A distinction you are trying to land (*"feeling vs. emotion in my relationship with my brother"*)
> - A piece of your Box you want to look at from outside (*"the rule that says I do not raise my voice"*)
> - A situation this week you are about to walk into (*"the conversation with my boss tomorrow"*)
> - A declaration you are considering (*"I am running the daily box catch for 30 days"*)
> - An experiment you are designing (*what / by when / what I will notice*)
>
> Say out loud what you are placing inside: *"I am placing inside the cube: [the piece]."*
>
> See it inside the cube. As a sentence, as a shape, as a memory, as a phrase. However your body wants to put it there.
>
> **Minutes 8–10 — look.** Stay centered, grounded, bubbled. Eyes soft. Attention on the cube.
>
> Do not solve. Do not figure out. Do not plan.
>
> Look.
>
> Notice what becomes visible about the piece that was not visible before you put it in the cube. Often: where the Box is hiding inside the piece. What ego state is running the piece. What feeling is underneath. What declaration would change everything. What experiment would test the smallest version.
>
> Do not write yet. Look. Two to three minutes.
>
> **Minute 11 — capture.** Open your notebook. Write fast, no editing. One to three sentences:
> - What became visible inside the cube
> - What the next move is — declaration, experiment, conversation, pause
> - What you are not yet ready to do
>
> Close the notebook.
>
> **Minute 12 — close the cube.** Out loud: *"The cube is closed."*
>
> The cube dissolves. The work stays.
>
> Confirm: *"I am centered. I am grounded. I am bubbled."*
>
> Three breaths. Drink water. Stand up. Go.

The cube is a workspace, not a refuge. Opened to construct or test. Closed when done. A learner who walks around with the cube permanently open is leaking — close it.

---

## 5. The dissolution script (8 minutes)

Run this at the end of any High module — Day 5, 6, 7, or 9 — to clear residue from the practice. The emotional body has been worked. The energetic body has held a charged container. Both want clearing before you return to ordinary life. Without this, you carry the module's residue into the next conversation, the next meal, the next sleep — and call that residue your mood.

> **Script. 8 minutes. Standing if you can, sitting if you must. Notebook open at the end.**
>
> **Minute 1 — confirm equipment.** Stand. Both feet on the floor. Center. Grounding cord. Bubble.
>
> Confirm out loud: *"I am centered. I am grounded. I am bubbled."*
>
> Three audible exhales.
>
> **Minute 2 — name what was worked.** Out loud, in one sentence: *"In this module, I worked [the piece]. What surfaced was [the feeling or material]."*
>
> Naming completes the practice. Until named, the practice has not finished.
>
> **Minutes 3–4 — discharge through the cord.** Bring attention to your center. Then to the cord.
>
> Out loud: *"Anything that is not mine, drain."*
>
> Feel — or imagine until you feel — the cord widening. Whatever you absorbed from the practice that is not yours moves down. Old emotional charge that surfaced and was not yet ready to process. The residue of your Box's noise. Tension in shoulders, jaw, gut. Allow.
>
> Sixty seconds, eyes soft, do nothing but allow.
>
> **Minute 5 — release the bubble's content.** The bubble has been the container for the practice. Now release its content.
>
> Out loud: *"Whatever entered my bubble in this practice that is not mine, leaves."*
>
> Imagine — or know — your bubble's edges becoming briefly permeable, then re-sealing. What was witness energy from your partner's earlier message, what was charge from the video, what was your Box's commentary — all releases.
>
> *"My bubble is set."* Re-confirm.
>
> **Minute 6 — physical movement.** Move the body. Shake hands and arms loosely for 30 seconds. Roll shoulders. Roll neck once each direction. Stretch jaw — yawn audibly, twice. Stamp feet ten times.
>
> Stored energy that did not move during the practice leaves now. The body completes what the practice started.
>
> **Minute 7 — drink and re-orient.** Drink a full glass of water, slowly. Cold water is fine. Hot water is fine.
>
> Look around the room. Name three things out loud. *"I see the lamp. I see the chair. I see the door."*
>
> Name what time of day it is. Name what you are about to do next.
>
> **Minute 8 — capture and close.** Notebook open. Write three lines, fast:
> - What I worked: ___
> - What surfaced: ___
> - What I am leaving in the practice (not carrying into the rest of the day): ___
>
> Close the notebook. Out loud: *"The practice is complete."*
>
> Walk away. The work is done. Trust that what wants to integrate will integrate while you do other things. Do not chew on it.

Run this after every High module. Skipping it is what makes Day 5 still being processed on Day 8. Run it.

---

## 6. The "I started crying and can't stop" emergency script (3 minutes)

Use the moment you notice the crying is not coming in waves with arrival, peak, and dissipation — it is looping, falling, or feels like there is no exit. This is emotion (per Day 5), not present-time sadness. The container needs to shift.

> **Script. 3 minutes. Read it aloud to yourself between sobs if you have to.**
>
> **Step 1 — interrupt.** Out loud: *"Stop."* Or: *"Pause."* Whatever word your body responds to.
>
> Stand up. If you cannot stand, sit upright and put both feet flat on the floor.
>
> **Step 2 — water on the face.** Get to the sink. Cold water — splash on your face. Once. Twice. Three times. The cold shocks the vagus nerve and resets the cry response. This is physiological, not punishment.
>
> Look in the mirror. See your own eyes for two seconds.
>
> **Step 3 — five senses.** Name out loud:
> - Five things you can see *("the mirror, the tap, the tile, my hand, the towel")*
> - Four things you can hear *("the tap dripping, my breath, the fridge, traffic")*
> - Three things you can feel *("water on my face, floor under my feet, the towel in my hand")*
> - Two things you can smell *("the soap, my own skin")*
> - One thing you can taste *("the inside of my mouth")*
>
> The crying will slow or stop during this. If it does not, repeat the five senses naming once more.
>
> **Step 4 — locate the emotion, do not process it.** Sit down. Put one hand on your chest, one on your belly.
>
> Out loud: *"This is an emotion. Not a present-time feeling. I am not processing it right now."*
>
> Name what age it might be from, if anything comes: *"This is old. This is from [a time, an age, a memory]."*
>
> Do not story. Do not figure out. Naming and stopping is the move.
>
> **Step 5 — partner message, then choose.** Pick up the phone. Voice message your pairing partner. Short: *"I hit an emotion in the module. I am grounded. I stopped. I am okay. I am letting you know."*
>
> Then decide:
> - Stop the module for today. Re-enter tomorrow.
> - If the crying restarts, run this script again, and message the CM.
> - If you have a therapist, this is a clean time to use a session. Be specific: *"I am doing a feelings-vs-emotions module. I hit an emotion. I am grounded. Can we work with it?"*
>
> Drink water. Move your body for two minutes — walk to another room, look out a window. Do not return to the module today.

The crying that loops is an open wound waiting for the right conditions to close. The right conditions are *not* finishing this module today. The right conditions are: ground, witness, professional support if needed, time. The course will be here tomorrow.

---

## 7. The "I went numb" emergency script (3 minutes)

Different shape from crying that won't stop. Numbness shows up as: you cannot feel anything in your body. You are watching the module from outside yourself. The words on the page are not landing. You notice you have stopped breathing fully. You feel a flatness that is somehow louder than feelings.

This is the Box re-installing the Numbness Bar in real time, because the previous practice loosened it. Numbness is information.

> **Script. 3 minutes.**
>
> **Step 1 — name it.** Out loud: *"I have gone numb. This is the Numbness Bar."*
>
> Naming brings consciousness to it. Without naming, you stay underneath.
>
> **Step 2 — move physically, hard.** Numbness will not lift from sitting still and trying to feel.
>
> Stand. Stamp your feet, hard, ten times. Hear the stamp.
>
> Press your palms together as hard as you can for ten seconds. Feel the resistance.
>
> Push against a wall — both hands flat against the wall, lean in, push with your legs, twenty seconds. Feel the muscles in your arms, shoulders, legs.
>
> Numbness lifts through pressure, not through softness.
>
> **Step 3 — find one sensation.** Sit back down. Center, ground, bubble — fast version.
>
> Pick one body location. Hands. Just your hands. Look at them.
>
> Out loud: *"In my hands right now, the sensation is [warm / cold / tingling / heavy / nothing yet — say nothing yet if that is what is true]."*
>
> If you found a sensation, locate one more — feet, or jaw, or chest. Name it.
>
> If you still feel nothing — drink cold water. Touch something with a sharp temperature. Hold an ice cube if you have one. Feel the cold. *Name the cold out loud.*
>
> **Step 4 — find one feeling at 2%.** Now, much smaller: *"In my emotional body, right now, at 2%, is there some anger, sadness, fear, or joy?"*
>
> Take whatever comes. *"Some fear, 2%, in my chest."* Or *"Some sadness, 3%, behind my eyes."* The 2% is enough. Do not chase a bigger version.
>
> If nothing comes after sixty seconds — that is data. Note it. The Numbness Bar is high today. That is information, not failure.
>
> **Step 5 — partner message, then decide.** Voice message your partner: *"I went numb in the module. I am grounded. I stopped. I will re-enter tomorrow."*
>
> Decide: stop the module today. Do something physical and grounding for an hour — walk outside, cook a real meal, take a hot shower, talk to another human about something unrelated. Numbness lifts through ordinary embodied life, not through more module.
>
> Re-enter the module tomorrow with Script 2 first.

Numbness is not failure. The Box did its old job — it cut the signal because the signal was going to be more than the system thought it could handle. You teach the system, slowly, that the signal is workable. The teaching is the work.

---

## 8. The "I'm in liquid state and don't know who I am" reorientation script (5 minutes)

The disorientation that arises mid-course is real, and it is normal. The Box has loosened enough that the old answer to *who am I* is not snapping back into place. The new answer has not formed yet. You are between thoughtwares. The feeling can be unmistakable — *I don't know what I think about anything anymore* — and is often confused for crisis. It is not crisis. It is liquid state. It is exactly what the course is for.

But you need to walk while you are in it, and walking requires equipment.

> **Script. 5 minutes.**
>
> **Minute 1 — name the state.** Out loud: *"I am in liquid state. This is not crisis. The old thoughtware is loose. The new thoughtware has not stabilized yet. This is what the course is for."*
>
> Naming relocates the disorientation from *something wrong with me* to *the predictable consequence of the work I am doing*.
>
> **Minute 2 — center, ground, bubble.** Fast version. Stand if you can.
>
> Center — hand on lower belly. *"My center is here."*
>
> Cord — *"My grounding cord is dropped."*
>
> Bubble — *"My bubble is set."*
>
> Three breaths.
>
> The equipment is what lets you stay in liquid state without flooding. Without the equipment, liquid state destabilizes. With it, liquid state works.
>
> **Minute 3 — what is still true.** Out loud, list what is still true. Things that have not become uncertain.
>
> *"My name is ___. I live in ___. The people I love today are ___. The work I do is ___. The body I am in is mine. The date is ___. I am taking the Expand the Box course and I am on Day ___."*
>
> If some of those are also currently in question, say only the ones that are not.
>
> The point is: there is a floor. The floor did not disappear. The Box was claiming credit for the floor — when the Box loosens, it feels like the floor is going too. It is not. Find the floor.
>
> **Minute 4 — what is in flux.** Out loud, name what is in flux. *"What is in flux right now is [my certainty about my job / my story about my marriage / my picture of who I am at work / my relationship with my parents]."*
>
> Naming it as flux distinguishes it from collapse. Flux is movement. Collapse is the absence of structure.
>
> *"This is liquid state in [the area]. I am letting the old thoughtware loosen. I am not yet rebuilding the new."*
>
> **Minute 5 — choose the next small thing.** Liquid state is not the time for large decisions. The decisions made in liquid state, sourced from disorientation, are usually the Box trying to grab any thoughtware to stop the discomfort of having none.
>
> Out loud: *"I will not make any large decisions in the next 48 hours. I will not quit, leave, end, or commit. I will pause."*
>
> Then choose **one** small concrete next thing. Drink water. Eat a meal. Walk for 20 minutes. Voice-message my partner. Sleep.
>
> Do that small thing. Then the next small thing. The thoughtware reformation happens on its own — your job is not to construct it on the spot. Your job is to *be alive while it happens*.

If the disorientation persists for more than 48 hours and does not respond to grounding, contact the CM. There is a difference between liquid state (workable with equipment) and a state that has spilled past what the course can hold (needs professional support). The CM helps you tell which.

---

## 9. Pre-sleep grounding for processing days (4 minutes)

Run this on the evening of any High module day, before you get into bed. The emotional and energetic body have been worked; sleep is when integration happens, but only if the system is not still spinning. This script signals to the system: *the work is complete for today, you may sleep now*.

> **Script. 4 minutes. Sitting on the edge of the bed, lights dim, phone away.**
>
> **Minute 1 — settle the room.** Put your phone in another room or face-down on do-not-disturb. Lights low. Water by the bed.
>
> Sit on the edge of the bed. Both feet on the floor.
>
> Three audible exhales, each longer than the inhale. Make them slow.
>
> **Minute 2 — center, ground, bubble.** Fast version, lying-down-ready.
>
> Center — hand on belly. *"My center is here."*
>
> Cord — *"My grounding cord is dropped. Anything that is not mine drains."*
>
> Bubble — *"My bubble is set. The day is outside it. The bedroom is the size of my bubble."*
>
> **Minute 3 — close the day.** Out loud, in one sentence each:
> - What I worked today: *"Today I worked [Day __, the practice, the partner exchange]."*
> - What surfaced: *"What surfaced was [the feeling / the realization / the question]."*
> - What is incomplete: *"What is incomplete and waiting is [name it, do not solve it]."*
> - What I am setting down for sleep: *"I am setting down [the worry, the conversation, the loop]. It will be there in the morning if it matters. I am letting sleep do its work."*
>
> Naming closes the loop. Unnamed loops keep running through the night.
>
> **Minute 4 — release the cord and rest.** Out loud: *"The work for today is complete."*
>
> Lie down. Feel the bed under you — full length of your body. Hands resting at your sides or on your belly.
>
> Center is still here. Grounding cord is still dropped — it does not turn off for sleep. Bubble is the size of the bed.
>
> Three slow breaths. Do not try to sleep. Let sleep come.
>
> If thought-loops start — name them: *"That is thought. I am not following it tonight."* Return attention to the cord, to the belly, to the breath.

The integration that happens in sleep after a processing day is real and large. The system is metabolizing what the module loosened. Protect the sleep — no scrolling, no late drink, no late food, no opening one more email. The pre-sleep ground is the move that says: *the day is closed; the integration can begin*.

---

## 10. Grounding outdoors (walking or sitting in nature)

Same principles, adapted for outside. Use when you are walking on processing days, or when you have stepped outside between module sections to clear your head. Many learners report the outdoor version is faster than indoor because the body is already in motion and the senses are already engaged.

> **Script. Variable length — works at 3 minutes or 30. Read it once, then go.**
>
> **First — choose your spot or your path.** A park. A street. A trail. A garden. Even a city block. The outdoor is what matters; it does not need to be wild.
>
> Phone in pocket, on silent. No earbuds. The point is to listen with the body.
>
> **Settle into the body.** Stand for thirty seconds. Both feet on the ground (or earth, or pavement — it works on pavement). Notice the contact through the soles.
>
> Three audible exhales.
>
> **Find your center while standing.** Hand on lower belly. *"My center is here."*
>
> Notice that center can be found while standing, while walking, anywhere. The Box wants to convince you grounding is only for sitting with eyes closed. It is not.
>
> **Drop a grounding cord through the ground.** *"My grounding cord is dropped."*
>
> The cord goes through the ground or pavement, through whatever is under it, into the earth. Pavement does not break it. The cord exists because you said so.
>
> Walking with a dropped cord feels different from walking without one. You will feel the difference in 30–60 seconds — a kind of weight in the lower body that was not there before, a steadiness.
>
> **Set the bubble — outdoors size.** Out loud or in your head: *"My bubble is set, arm's length."*
>
> Outdoors the bubble is the same size as indoors. Resist the pull to make it bigger because you are in open space. Arm's length keeps it operational.
>
> **Begin walking, or sit if you are sitting.** Now use the senses.
>
> *Three things you can see, named in your head as you walk: "tree, fence, sky."*
>
> *Two things you can hear: "bird, car."*
>
> *One thing you can smell: "grass, wet pavement, rain."*
>
> If you are walking, match your breath to your stride for a few minutes. Inhale three steps, exhale four. Find the count your body wants.
>
> **Notice the feeling in the body.** *"In my emotional body, right now, at low intensity, is there some anger, sadness, fear, joy?"*
>
> Outdoors, feelings often arrive that have been blocked indoors. The open space gives them room. Name what arrives, locate it, do not story it. Keep walking.
>
> **Close when you return.** Before you come back inside, pause at the door. *"I am centered. I am grounded. I am bubbled. I am returning indoors."*
>
> If you sat — same close, before you stand to leave.

The outdoor practice does not replace the indoor one. It runs alongside it. The combination — daily indoor practice plus regular outdoor grounding — is what installs the energetic-body equipment most reliably across the 30 days of the course and the 90 days after.

---

## 11. Final note

These eleven scripts are the equipment. You do not have to do all of them every day. You have to know which one to reach for in the moment you need it.

Three rules:

1. **Run as written before you customize.** The scripts have been tuned. They land cleanly if read as written. Customizations are best made after 30 days of as-written practice, not before.
2. **Read them aloud or record them.** PM is a body practice. Scripts read silently land in the head. Scripts read aloud — or played back in your own voice — land in the body. The difference is large.
3. **Do not skip the practice because you are "fine."** *Fine* is often the Box. The practice is the rep. The rep is the work.

When you come back to this file in Week 4, you will find some of the scripts you needed in Week 2 you no longer need — the equipment has installed. New scripts (3, 4, 5) will be the ones you use most. That is the arc.

Welcome to your own equipment.

| **For** | The operator standing up a cohort (and any contributor wondering what a video filename in the v1 sources referred to) |
| **What it is** | The complete inventory of every video the v1 course cited, its rights status, and the operator action per slot — plus the orientation-video slot list an operator would record fresh |
| **Reads alongside** | `02 - Async Delivery Framework.md` (module artifacts) · `01 - Course Architecture and Pedagogy.md` (the text-first decision) |

## The v2 posture (read this before the table)

**Every module in this course is complete as text.** The written module file is the single canonical source for its module; the maps, practices, scripts, and tools all ship in full. **Videos are optional enrichment.** A learner who never watches a single video misses style and company, not content. No learner-facing file in v2 references a video by bare filename; learner-facing text says "videos are enrichment" and points here, and this manifest is the only place filenames live.

This resolves v1's dead end: v1 module headers cited files like `06 - LiquidState_EN.mp4` that no learner could click, stream, or locate. Those filenames referred to **private recordings from a live cohort, held by the course owner, outside the CC BY-SA grant that covers the written course.** They were never shippable, and one slot (08) never existed at all.

What this means for an operator, in one paragraph: you can run a full cohort with zero videos. If you want video, you have two routes per slot below: **obtain** (license or get permission from the rights holder to stream the private recording to your cohort) or **record** (make your own replacement; the module text is script-grade and liftable). Either way, host on a platform meeting the privacy minimums in Doc 04 §H, and link videos from your cohort platform, not from the course files.

## B. Orientation videos — the set an operator records fresh

Doc 02 lists a short per-module orientation video (3–5 minutes) as the first module artifact. These never existed in v1 either; they are the cheapest set to produce because **the scripts are liftable straight from the module intros** — each module's purpose section, intensity tag, pace recommendation, and safety pointer, read warmly by the person the cohort will actually be hearing from (operator or CM). One afternoon, twelve takes, done.

Per video, cover four beats: what this module is for (lift from the module's purpose paragraph) · the intensity tag and what it asks (time, partner reachability for High modules) · where the safety equipment is (grounding script, pause rules) · one sentence of pace honesty ("this one is heavier; take the pause day after").

| Slot | Suggested filename | Covers | Special notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| O-00 | `orient-m00-welcome.mp4` | Module 00 | Doubles as the cohort welcome. Name the CM, the feed, the calendar, the refund tree's existence. |
| O-01 | `orient-m01.mp4` | Module 01 | Mention the red-pill ceremony is done solo, out loud. |
| O-02 | `orient-m02.mp4` | Module 02 | |
| O-03 | `orient-m03.mp4` | Module 03 | Name Script 3 becoming the standing daily practice. |
| O-04 | `orient-m04.mp4` | Module 04 | Name the Beep! Book grammar upgrade. |
| O-05 | `orient-m05.mp4` | Module 05 | First High module: walk the unlock checklist on camera. |
| O-06 | `orient-m06.mp4` | Module 06 | Heavier than any other module. State the 40% hard stop and the witness scope plainly; announce the consolidation pause day after. |
| O-07 | `orient-m07.mp4` | Module 07 | High. Note that Modules 8–9 still lie ahead (do not call the climb over). |
| O-08 | `orient-m08.mp4` | Module 08 | The partner exchange is the module; tell pairs to schedule 45 minutes now. Announce the rest day after. |
| O-09 | `orient-m09.mp4` | Module 09 | High. Name the Demon-locator boundary in one sentence. |
| O-10 | `orient-m10.mp4` | Module 10 | |
| O-11 | `orient-m11.mp4` | Module 11 | Closing: the witnessed declaration, the 90-day container, the Possibility Team option, the anonymous self-assessment window. |

Recording guidance: one take per video is fine; warmth beats polish. Face on camera, ordinary room, no slides (the maps live in the modules). Keep each under five minutes; if a script runs long, the module text already says it better. Read the safety sentences as written in the module file, not from memory.

For guided *audio* of the practice scripts (a separate, equally cheap production task), see the [Audio Companion Recording Kit](Audio%20Companion%20Recording%20Kit.md).
